statement: Spring Boot's core value proposition is convention over configuration: the framework makes opinionated default decisions so developers write zero XML and minimal properties files for standard use cases. If spring-data-jpa is on the classpath and a DataSource is configured, Spring Boot auto-configures an EntityManagerFactory, TransactionManager, and JPA repositories — all without a single @Bean declaration. The philosophy shift from Spring Framework is that opt-out (disable auto-config via exclude) is easier than opt-in (write configuration from scratch). This is why a production-ready REST service can start in 15 lines of code, and why the learning curve pitfall is not in starting but in understanding what the framework did implicitly when things go wrong.

Spring's Inversion of Control (IoC) container is its architectural foundation: instead of objects creating their own dependencies (new MyService()), the container creates and wires everything. Dependency Injection is the mechanism — objects declare what they need via constructor parameters, fields, or setters, and the container satisfies those needs. This inversion makes unit testing trivial (pass mocks in the constructor), decouples components (depend on interfaces, not implementations), and centralizes lifecycle management. Spring recommends constructor injection over field injection: constructor injection makes dependencies explicit, enables immutable fields (final), and surfaces circular dependencies at startup rather than at runtime.

Spring Boot auto-configuration works via the @EnableAutoConfiguration mechanism: at startup, Spring Boot reads META-INF/spring/org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.AutoConfiguration.imports (Spring Boot 3.x) or META-INF/spring.factories (Spring Boot 2.x) to discover all @AutoConfiguration classes. Each class uses @ConditionalOnClass, @ConditionalOnMissingBean, and @ConditionalOnProperty annotations to apply configuration only when conditions are met. The order of auto-configurations matters and is controlled by @AutoConfigureBefore / @AutoConfigureAfter. To debug what was applied and why: run with --debug flag or set logging.level.org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure=DEBUG, which prints the AutoConfiguration report (CONDITIONS EVALUATION REPORT) listing every candidate and its match/no-match reason.

Circular dependency injection (A depends on B, B depends on A) causes BeanCurrentlyInCreationException at startup with constructor injection — Spring cannot satisfy both at the same time. With field injection (@Autowired), Spring historically resolved this silently using three-level cache (early-reference singletons), masking design problems. Spring Boot 2.6+ made circular dependencies a startup failure by default (spring.main.allow-circular-references defaults to false) precisely to force architectural fixes. The correct fix is redesign: extract a third component C that both A and B depend on, or use @Lazy on one injection point as a last resort (defers proxy resolution but does not fix the underlying coupling). Seeing this error is a signal that the service boundary is wrong.

@Transactional works through Spring AOP proxies: when you inject a @Service bean, you get a proxy, and calling a @Transactional method through the proxy triggers transaction management. But if a method inside the same class calls another @Transactional method on `this`, the call bypasses the proxy entirely — the inner method runs without a transaction (or inherits whatever the outer context is). This is the most notorious Spring bug: developers add @Transactional to an internal method, see no error, but no transaction is created. Fixes: (a) move the inner method to a separate @Service bean and inject it; (b) inject the bean into itself via @Autowired (self-injection) — ugly but works; (c) use AspectJ weaving instead of proxy-based AOP. The root cause is proxy-based AOP's fundamental limitation: it only intercepts calls that cross the proxy boundary.

Spring Security is implemented as a chain of servlet filters registered with a specific order. The SecurityFilterChain defines which URLs are intercepted and which filters apply. A critical ordering gotcha: if you define multiple SecurityFilterChain beans (common for separating API auth from web form auth), they must have explicit @Order annotations — lower number = higher priority. Without explicit ordering, both chains match all requests and behavior is undefined. Common pattern: @Order(1) for JWT/API chain that matches /api/**, @Order(2) for form-login chain that matches everything else. Also important: filters run before @ControllerAdvice exception handling, so security exceptions (401/403) must be handled via AuthenticationEntryPoint and AccessDeniedHandler, not @ExceptionHandler.

The JPA N+1 query problem: if you fetch a list of 100 Order entities and each Order has a lazy-loaded List<OrderItem>, accessing orderItem fields in a loop triggers 100 additional SELECT queries (1 for orders + N for items). This is silent — no exception, just catastrophic performance at scale. Solutions in priority order: (1) JPQL JOIN FETCH: `SELECT o FROM Order o JOIN FETCH o.items` — fetches everything in one query; (2) @EntityGraph on the repository method — declarative join fetch without modifying JPQL; (3) @BatchSize(size=N) — fetches N collections per batch, reducing N+1 to ceil(N/batch)+1 queries; (4) projections/DTOs via constructor expressions — bypass entity graph entirely when you only need a subset of fields. The general rule: never leave bidirectional associations as FetchType.LAZY without a strategy for the collection access pattern.

Spring Boot Actuator exposes production-readiness endpoints over HTTP or JMX. Key endpoints: /actuator/health (liveness/readiness — critical for Kubernetes probes), /actuator/metrics (Micrometer integration for Prometheus), /actuator/info (build/git metadata), /actuator/env (resolved configuration values), /actuator/loggers (change log level at runtime without restart), /actuator/threaddump and /actuator/heapdump for debugging live issues. Security note: Actuator endpoints are sensitive — expose only /health and /info publicly; protect the rest behind management.server.port on a non-public network interface. The management.endpoints.web.exposure.include property defaults to only 'health' in production; explicit opt-in prevents accidentally exposing internal state. Actuator's Micrometer integration is the preferred path to metrics rather than custom instrumentation.

Spring Boot's profile-based configuration allows environment-specific properties via application-{profile}.properties (or .yml). Active profiles are set via SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE env var, --spring.profiles.active command-line arg, or spring.profiles.active in application.properties. Key design principles: (1) application.properties holds universal defaults; profile files override only what differs; (2) multiple profiles can be active simultaneously (comma-separated), with later profiles winning on conflict; (3) @Profile on @Configuration/@Bean controls which beans exist in each environment; (4) Spring Boot 2.4+ introduced config trees and importable config files — avoid mixing old-style spring.profiles with new-style spring.config.activate.on-profile. Credentials should never be in profile files; use environment variables or Spring Cloud Config / Vault integration.

Spring MVC (servlet stack) uses one thread per request from a thread pool; threads block during I/O (DB calls, HTTP calls). Spring WebFlux (reactive stack) uses an event loop with non-blocking I/O; a small number of threads handle thousands of concurrent requests without blocking. The correct choice: use WebFlux when your application is genuinely I/O-bound and you need high concurrency with minimal threads (API gateway, streaming, chat), or when integrating with reactive data stores (R2DBC, Reactive MongoDB). Use MVC when: your team is unfamiliar with reactive programming (debugging a Flux stack trace is significantly harder), your data store is JPA/JDBC (no reactive driver), or you use Spring Security features not yet ported to WebFlux. Mixing the stacks is possible but discouraged. Reactive does not automatically mean faster — on low-concurrency CRUD apps it adds overhead for zero gain.

Spring Bean lifecycle order: constructor → @Autowired field/setter injection → @PostConstruct → ApplicationListener<ContextRefreshedEvent>. Common ordering pitfall: @PostConstruct runs before the full application context is ready, so beans that use ApplicationEventPublisher or interact with other beans that have their own @PostConstruct may encounter uninitialized state. Prefer implementing SmartInitializingSingleton (afterSingletonsInstantiated()) when you need all singletons to be initialized first. For startup tasks, ApplicationRunner and CommandLineRunner run after the full context is refreshed, making them the safest hooks for data seeding, cache warming, or external service handshakes. @PreDestroy runs during graceful shutdown — use for connection cleanup, but set spring.lifecycle.timeout-per-shutdown-phase to give it enough time.

Spring Boot starters (spring-boot-starter-web, spring-boot-starter-data-jpa, etc.) are dependency aggregators — they declare transitive dependencies at versions known to work together, managed by the Spring Boot BOM (Bill of Materials) via spring-boot-dependencies. This means you rarely specify versions for Spring ecosystem libraries. The trade-off: (1) version conflicts arise when you mix Spring Boot-managed libraries with third-party libraries that have their own version constraints — use `mvn dependency:tree` or `gradle dependencies` to trace conflicts; (2) upgrading Spring Boot major version (2.x→3.x) can cascade breaking changes: Jakarta EE namespace (javax→jakarta), removal of deprecated APIs, Hibernate 6 dialect changes. The discipline is: let the BOM manage versions, override only when you have a documented reason, and always run integration tests after BOM upgrades.

Spring WebFlux reactive pipelines silently block the event loop when code calls a blocking method—JDBC, Files.readAllBytes, Thread.sleep—inside a Mono/Flux operator. The event loop has a fixed small thread pool, so one blocking call stalls all concurrent requests on that thread. The failure is invisible under low load and catastrophic at moderate concurrency. The correct pattern is to wrap all blocking calls in Schedulers.boundedElastic() via publishOn or subscribeOn. Tools like BlockHound can detect blocking calls in reactive context at test time and should be enabled in CI.

Spring Security's CSRF protection is enabled by default for stateful (session-based) apps but disabled by default for stateless (JWT/API) apps when using the lambda DSL. The hidden failure is that teams extending the wrong SecurityFilterChain base class or missing the csrf.disable() call either block legitimate API clients with 403 CSRF errors or unintentionally expose session-based endpoints to CSRF attacks. The discipline is to always explicitly declare CSRF policy—either csrf.disable() for pure APIs or csrfTokenRepository for session apps—rather than relying on defaults that change between Spring Security major versions.

JPA's @OneToOne with fetch = LAZY is silently treated as EAGER when the association is on the owning side without a @LazyToOne hint. Hibernate cannot make a lazy proxy for the owning side of a @OneToOne because it must check whether the associated row exists. The consequence is N+1 loads for a relationship developers believe is lazy. The fix is to use Bytecode Enhancement or restructure to @OneToMany with a unique constraint when lazy loading on a @OneToOne owning side is performance-critical.

@SpringBootTest starts the full application context including all beans, auto-configuration, and embedded server, making it the most realistic but slowest test type. The correct layered testing strategy is: @WebMvcTest for controller layer only (security, validation, serialization), @DataJpaTest for repository layer only (actual SQL via H2 or Testcontainers), and @SpringBootTest only for integration tests verifying cross-layer behavior. Each slice annotation loads a restricted context subset. Overusing @SpringBootTest for what should be unit tests creates test suites that take minutes and fail for unrelated bean initialization reasons.

GraalVM Native Image compilation for Spring Boot 3 requires all reflective access, proxy generation, and resource loading to be declared up front via @RegisterReflectionForBinding, @ImportRuntimeHints, or native-image.properties. The hidden payoff is startup times under 100ms and minimal memory footprint—critical for serverless and sidecar deployments. The hidden cost is that third-party libraries that use reflection without providing native hints break at runtime, not compile time. The discipline is to run the full test suite against the native binary in CI and to check Spring's third-party library compatibility matrix before upgrading.

Testcontainers with Spring Boot allows integration tests to spin up real databases, Kafka brokers, or Redis instances in Docker. The hidden failure is that each test class annotated with @SpringBootTest creates a new ApplicationContext and potentially a new container unless @DynamicPropertySource and container reuse are configured correctly. Without reuse, a test suite with 20 integration test classes starts 20 Postgres containers sequentially, adding minutes to CI. The correct pattern is to use a shared static container with Testcontainers' reuse flag or Spring Boot 3.1's ServiceConnection auto-configuration which handles container lifecycle sharing automatically.

Spring's @Async annotation creates a fire-and-forget proxy call but silently swallows exceptions unless a custom AsyncUncaughtExceptionHandler is configured. The default behavior logs the exception at ERROR level and discards it. Business operations marked @Async—sending emails, audit logging, notifications—fail without any caller visibility. The correct pattern is to always configure an AsyncUncaughtExceptionHandler that persists failures to a retry queue or alerts, and to return CompletableFuture<Void> from @Async methods in contexts where the caller needs to know about failure.

Spring Boot's default connection pool (HikariCP) has a default pool size of 10 connections. Under moderate load, a service making synchronous database calls from a thread-per-request model saturates the pool and queues threads waiting for connections. The hidden constraint is that increasing pool size is not always the correct fix: oversized pools can overwhelm the database. The correct analysis is to measure connection wait time in Actuator's hikaricp.connections.pending metric, ensure database-side max_connections is at least sum of all service pool sizes, and consider read replicas or caching before simply raising pool limits.

Spring Batch is the correct framework for large-scale ETL in Spring Boot applications because it provides chunk-oriented processing, restartability from the last successful checkpoint, and job execution metadata out of the box. The hidden pattern is using StepExecutionListener and ItemProcessor pipelines to keep transformation logic testable in isolation. Teams that implement large data migrations as @Scheduled methods or @Async tasks discover after the first failure that they have no restart capability, no partial progress tracking, and no retry semantics—exactly what Spring Batch provides by default.

Micrometer metrics registered with @Timed or Timer.record() require the application to have at least one request or method invocation before the metric appears in Prometheus or Datadog. Newly deployed services that have not yet received traffic show empty dashboards, not zero-value metrics. Alerting rules that fire on missing metrics will page immediately after a clean deploy. The correct pattern is to eagerly register all meters at startup with a zero value using MeterRegistry.gauge() or counter() initialization, and to document this behavior in runbook so on-call engineers understand that missing metrics at startup are expected.

FAILURE: Spring `@Transactional` self-invocation (same-class method call) bypasses the proxy and the transaction. Spring `@Transactional` works via CGLIB proxy — when method A in the same class calls `@Transactional` method B directly (this.B()), the call goes through `this`, not through the proxy, and no transaction is created. Symptoms: database writes in method B are not rolled back on exception, no transaction demarcation. Fix: (1) inject the bean into itself via `@Autowired private MyService self` and call `self.B()`; (2) extract method B into a separate `@Component`; (3) use `AopContext.currentProxy()` with `@EnableAspectJAutoProxy(exposeProxy=true)` — least preferred. UNSAID: this is the most common Spring `@Transactional` mistake. It also affects other AOP-based annotations: `@Cacheable`, `@Async`, `@Retry`. The root cause is that Spring AOP is proxy-based, not bytecode-instrumented at the call site (unlike AspectJ weaving). Self-injection (option 1) introduces a circular dependency — Spring handles it by allowing circular dependencies in singleton beans but this can mask initialization order bugs. Extracting to a separate service (option 2) is cleanest.

FAILURE: JPA/Hibernate N+1 query problem with lazy-loaded associations. Fetching a list of 100 entities with a `@ManyToOne` or `@OneToMany` association triggers 1 query for the list + 100 individual queries for each related entity. Symptoms: slow page loads, high database query count in logs, query count proportional to result set size. Fix: (1) JPQL JOIN FETCH: `SELECT DISTINCT u FROM User u JOIN FETCH u.orders WHERE u.active = true`; (2) `@EntityGraph(attributePaths = {"orders"})` on repository method; (3) Spring Data `@Query` with explicit join; (4) DTO projection with JPQL constructor expression. UNSAID: `@OneToMany(fetch = FetchType.EAGER)` does not fix N+1 — it makes it worse by loading associations in every query even when not needed, and causes Cartesian products for multiple eager collections. Detecting N+1: enable SQL logging `spring.jpa.show-sql=true` or use `hibernate.generate_statistics=true`. In tests: `Hibernate Statistics` or `datasource-proxy` library counts queries. `Blaze-Persistence` or `Spring Data Projections` for efficient DTO fetching. The Hibernate second-level cache can mitigate N+1 for reference data but does not solve it for volatile associations.

Spring Boot `@Async` configuration for non-blocking method execution: annotate method with `@Async`, enable with `@EnableAsync` on a `@Configuration` class. Configure a custom `TaskExecutor` to avoid `SimpleAsyncTaskExecutor` (which creates a new thread per call with no limit): `@Bean public TaskExecutor taskExecutor() { ThreadPoolTaskExecutor ex = new ThreadPoolTaskExecutor(); ex.setCorePoolSize(5); ex.setMaxPoolSize(20); ex.setQueueCapacity(100); ex.setRejectedExecutionHandler(new ThreadPoolExecutor.CallerRunsPolicy()); ex.initialize(); return ex; }`. UNSAID: `@Async` without a custom executor uses `SimpleAsyncTaskExecutor` by default, which creates a new thread per call — catastrophic under load. Exception handling in `@Async` methods: exceptions are swallowed unless the method returns `Future<T>` or `CompletableFuture<T>` — callers must check `.get()` or use `AsyncUncaughtExceptionHandler`. `@Async` with `void` return silently swallows all exceptions — always return `CompletableFuture<Void>`. Thread pool sizing: for I/O-bound tasks, size to expected concurrent I/O operations; for CPU-bound, size to `Runtime.getRuntime().availableProcessors()`.

Spring Boot HikariCP connection pool tuning: `spring.datasource.hikari.maximum-pool-size=20` (default 10). `spring.datasource.hikari.minimum-idle=5`. `spring.datasource.hikari.connection-timeout=30000` (30s, fail fast if pool exhausted). `spring.datasource.hikari.idle-timeout=600000` (10m). `spring.datasource.hikari.max-lifetime=1800000` (30m, shorter than database `wait_timeout`). `spring.datasource.hikari.keepalive-time=300000` (5m, prevents idle connection closure by network equipment). `spring.datasource.hikari.pool-name=MyPool`. UNSAID: pool size formula (from HikariCP docs): `connections = (core_count * 2) + effective_spindle_count`. For most modern SSD-backed databases: `maxPoolSize = cpu_cores * 2 + 1`. Too many connections waste memory and cause PostgreSQL to spend time in connection management. `connection-timeout` is the time an application waits for a connection from the pool — set to your request timeout budget. `max-lifetime` must be less than the database's `wait_timeout`/`interactive_timeout` by at least 30 seconds. Enable HikariCP metrics via Micrometer: `management.metrics.enable.hikaricp=true`. Slow query warnings: `spring.jpa.properties.hibernate.format_sql=true` combined with `p6spy` or `datasource-proxy`.

Spring Security JWT authentication configuration (Spring Security 6+): `@Bean SecurityFilterChain filterChain(HttpSecurity http) throws Exception { http.csrf(AbstractHttpConfigurer::disable).sessionManagement(s -> s.sessionCreationPolicy(SessionCreationPolicy.STATELESS)).authorizeHttpRequests(auth -> auth.requestMatchers("/api/auth/**").permitAll().anyRequest().authenticated()).addFilterBefore(jwtAuthFilter, UsernamePasswordAuthenticationFilter.class); return http.build(); }`. JWT filter: extract token from `Authorization: Bearer <token>` header, validate with JJWT or `nimbus-jose-jwt`, set `SecurityContextHolder` with `UsernamePasswordAuthenticationToken`. UNSAID: in Spring Security 6, the configuration API changed significantly — `WebSecurityConfigurerAdapter` is removed, use `SecurityFilterChain` beans instead. `STATELESS` session policy disables HTTP sessions — no `JSESSIONID` cookie, reducing CSRF attack surface. JWT secret must be at least 256 bits for HS256. For RS256: use RSA key pair, distribute public key to resource servers. `JwtDecoder` bean with `NimbusJwtDecoder` for OAuth2 resource server: `http.oauth2ResourceServer(oauth2 -> oauth2.jwt(jwt -> jwt.decoder(jwtDecoder)))` — simpler than manual filter. Validate `exp`, `iss`, and `aud` claims in every token verification.

Spring Boot Actuator production configuration: enable only necessary endpoints: `management.endpoints.web.exposure.include=health,info,metrics,prometheus`. Secure management port: `management.server.port=8081` (separate from app port 8080). Health indicators: `management.endpoint.health.show-details=when_authorized`. Custom health indicator: implement `HealthIndicator` interface: `@Component class DatabaseHealthIndicator implements HealthIndicator { public Health health() { try { jdbcTemplate.queryForObject("SELECT 1", Integer.class); return Health.up().build(); } catch (Exception e) { return Health.down(e).build(); } } }`. UNSAID: never expose all actuator endpoints publicly — `/actuator/env` shows all configuration including secrets, `/actuator/heapdump` dumps the entire JVM heap. Restrict with Spring Security: require ACTUATOR role for management endpoints. `prometheus` endpoint (requires `micrometer-registry-prometheus` dependency) exposes metrics in Prometheus format for scraping. For Kubernetes: expose `/actuator/health/liveness` and `/actuator/health/readiness` separately — Spring Boot 2.3+ provides these out of the box. Liveness: JVM is alive. Readiness: application is ready to serve (connections established, caches warmed).

Spring Boot `@ConfigurationProperties` for type-safe configuration binding: `@ConfigurationProperties(prefix = "app.service") @Validated public class ServiceConfig { @NotNull @Min(1) @Max(60) private Duration timeout = Duration.ofSeconds(30); @NotEmpty private String apiKey; @Valid private RetryConfig retry = new RetryConfig(); }`. Register: `@EnableConfigurationProperties(ServiceConfig.class)` or add `@Component`. `application.yml`: `app.service.timeout: 30s; app.service.api-key: ${APP_API_KEY}`. UNSAID: `@ConfigurationProperties` is preferable to `@Value` for multiple related properties — it groups them, supports validation, and is IDE-friendly (autocompletion in `.yml`). `@Validated` enables JSR-303 validation — bind failures throw `BindException` at startup, preventing the app from starting with invalid config. For secrets: use `@Value("${APP_SECRET}")` for single values from env vars, or integrate with Spring Cloud Config / HashiCorp Vault for centralized secret management. `@ConfigurationPropertiesScan` auto-detects all `@ConfigurationProperties` classes. Duration binding: Spring Boot automatically converts `30s`, `5m`, `2h` strings to `java.time.Duration`.

Spring Boot Resilience4j circuit breaker integration: dependency `spring-boot-starter-aop` + `resilience4j-spring-boot3`. Annotate: `@CircuitBreaker(name = "externalApi", fallbackMethod = "fallback")`. Configure in `application.yml`: `resilience4j.circuitbreaker.instances.externalApi.slidingWindowSize=10; failureRateThreshold=50; waitDurationInOpenState=30s; permittedNumberOfCallsInHalfOpenState=3`. Fallback: `private ResponseEntity<T> fallback(Exception ex) { return ResponseEntity.status(503).build(); }`. UNSAID: Resilience4j also provides `@Retry`, `@TimeLimiter`, `@Bulkhead`, `@RateLimiter` — compose them: `@CircuitBreaker @Retry @TimeLimiter`. Execution order (outermost to innermost): `Bulkhead -> TimeLimiter -> CircuitBreaker -> Retry`. For reactive: annotate Reactor/RxJava publishers with the same annotations. `management.health.circuitbreakers.enabled=true` exposes circuit breaker state via Actuator. `sliding-window-type: COUNT_BASED` vs `TIME_BASED` — count-based for low-traffic services (N last calls), time-based for high-traffic (calls in last N seconds). Fallback method must have the same return type and accept the exception as the last parameter.

Spring Data JPA repository patterns: extend `JpaRepository<Entity, ID>` for CRUD + pagination + sorting. Derived query methods: `findByEmailAndActive(String email, boolean active)`. JPQL: `@Query("SELECT u FROM User u WHERE u.createdAt > :date") List<User> findRecent(@Param("date") LocalDateTime date)`. Native SQL: `@Query(value = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE ...", nativeQuery = true)`. Pagination: `Page<User> findByActive(boolean active, Pageable pageable)`. Call: `repository.findByActive(true, PageRequest.of(0, 20, Sort.by("createdAt").descending()))`. UNSAID: `Page<T>` includes total count (requires an extra COUNT query) — for UI pagination, this is needed. For infinite scroll / cursor pagination, use `Slice<T>` which does not count total — just checks if there's a next page (one less query). `@Modifying` required for `@Query` UPDATE/DELETE statements. `@Transactional` on `@Modifying` queries. `findAll(Specification<T>)` with `JpaSpecificationExecutor` for dynamic queries. Spring Data Projections: `interface UserSummary { String getName(); String getEmail(); }` as return type for partial-load queries.

Spring Boot exception handling with `@ControllerAdvice`: `@RestControllerAdvice public class GlobalExceptionHandler { @ExceptionHandler(ResourceNotFoundException.class) @ResponseStatus(HttpStatus.NOT_FOUND) public ErrorResponse handleNotFound(ResourceNotFoundException ex) { return new ErrorResponse("NOT_FOUND", ex.getMessage()); } @ExceptionHandler(MethodArgumentNotValidException.class) @ResponseStatus(HttpStatus.BAD_REQUEST) public ErrorResponse handleValidation(MethodArgumentNotValidException ex) { List<String> errors = ex.getBindingResult().getFieldErrors().stream().map(fe -> fe.getField() + ": " + fe.getDefaultMessage()).toList(); return new ErrorResponse("VALIDATION_FAILED", errors); } @ExceptionHandler(Exception.class) @ResponseStatus(HttpStatus.INTERNAL_SERVER_ERROR) public ErrorResponse handleGeneric(Exception ex, HttpServletRequest request) { log.error("Unhandled exception for {}", request.getRequestURI(), ex); return new ErrorResponse("INTERNAL_ERROR", "An unexpected error occurred"); } }`. UNSAID: `@RestControllerAdvice` is `@ControllerAdvice` + `@ResponseBody`. Order: more specific exceptions should be handled before generic `Exception`. Never expose internal exception messages to clients — log full details server-side, return sanitized messages. `ProblemDetail` (RFC 7807) is available in Spring 6+ for standardized error responses: `return ProblemDetail.forStatusAndDetail(HttpStatus.NOT_FOUND, message)`.

Spring Boot `@Cacheable` with Redis: dependency `spring-boot-starter-data-redis` + `spring-boot-starter-cache`. Configure: `@EnableCaching` on a `@Configuration` class. `spring.cache.type=redis; spring.data.redis.host=localhost`. TTL per cache: `@Bean RedisCacheManagerBuilderCustomizer cacheCustomizer() { return builder -> builder.withCacheConfiguration("users", RedisCacheConfiguration.defaultCacheConfig().entryTtl(Duration.ofMinutes(10))); }`. Use: `@Cacheable(value="users", key="#id") User findById(Long id)`. Evict: `@CacheEvict(value="users", key="#user.id") void update(User user)`. UNSAID: `@Cacheable` key generation: default uses all method parameters, but define explicit keys to avoid collisions. Use SpEL for composite keys: `key="'user:' + #id + ':' + #tenantId"`. `@CachePut` updates the cache without skipping method execution (for write-through). Cache stampede (cache miss under high load): use `sync=true` on `@Cacheable` to use a lock — only one thread calls the method, others wait. Redis serialization: default is JDK serialization (fragile) — configure `RedisSerializer.json()` with a `TypeReference` for type-safe JSON serialization. Cache aside vs write-through: `@Cacheable` implements cache aside.

Spring Boot virtual threads (Project Loom) with Spring 6.1+ / Spring Boot 3.2+: `spring.threads.virtual.enabled=true` — all Tomcat request-handling threads become virtual threads. For `@Async`: `@Bean TaskExecutor taskExecutor() { return new TaskExecutorAdapter(Executors.newVirtualThreadPerTaskExecutor()); }`. For scheduled tasks: `@Bean TaskScheduler taskScheduler() { ThreadPoolTaskScheduler ts = new ThreadPoolTaskScheduler(); ts.setVirtualThreadsEnabled(true); return ts; }`. UNSAID: virtual threads are managed by the JVM, not the OS — creating millions is inexpensive (no OS thread stack allocation). This eliminates the need for reactive programming (WebFlux) for most I/O-bound workloads — blocking code is fine with virtual threads. Do NOT use thread locals as cache keyed to virtual threads — virtual threads are ephemeral and thread locals won't persist. `synchronized` blocks pin virtual threads to OS threads — replace with `ReentrantLock` for code that needs to run in virtual threads without pinning. Profiling: `jdk.VirtualThreadPinned` JFR event shows pinning locations. Spring WebFlux is still better for backpressure-aware streaming workloads.

Spring Boot `@Scheduled` for periodic tasks: `@Scheduled(fixedRate = 60_000)` — run every 60s from start of previous execution (overlapping if slow). `@Scheduled(fixedDelay = 60_000)` — run 60s after previous execution completes (non-overlapping). `@Scheduled(cron = "0 0 * * * *")` — cron expression (hourly). `@Scheduled(cron = "${app.job.cron:0 0 * * * *}")` — configurable cron. `@EnableScheduling` on `@Configuration`. UNSAID: `fixedRate` can cause concurrent executions if a task runs longer than the rate — add `@Async` or use `fixedDelay` instead. For distributed systems: `@Scheduled` runs on every instance — use ShedLock (`net.javacrumbs.shedlock`) to ensure only one instance runs the job: `@SchedulerLock(name="importJob", lockAtMostFor="10m", lockAtLeastFor="1m")`. Without distributed locking, N instances each running a scheduled job causes N times the intended side effects. Cron timezone: `@Scheduled(cron = "...", zone = "UTC")` — always specify timezone to avoid DST issues. Spring batch for complex multi-step jobs with retry and skip logic.

Spring Boot Testcontainers integration tests: dependency `spring-boot-testcontainers` (Spring Boot 3.1+). `@SpringBootTest @Testcontainers class UserServiceIT { @Container static PostgreSQLContainer<?> postgres = new PostgreSQLContainer<>("postgres:16"); @DynamicPropertySource static void props(DynamicPropertyRegistry r) { r.add("spring.datasource.url", postgres::getJdbcUrl); r.add("spring.datasource.username", postgres::getUsername); r.add("spring.datasource.password", postgres::getPassword); } }`. Spring Boot 3.1+ service connection: `@ServiceConnection PostgreSQLContainer<?> postgres = new PostgreSQLContainer<>("postgres:16")` — auto-configures datasource. UNSAID: Testcontainers spin up real Docker containers — tests are slower but test real database behavior (constraints, indexes, query plans). `@Container static` reuses the container across all tests in the class. `@Container` (non-static) creates a new container per test — very slow. For faster tests: use `@Container static` and `@Sql` annotations to reset data between tests. `@TestConfiguration` with `@ServiceConnection` in a shared base class. Docker must be available in CI — use Testcontainers Cloud or GitHub Actions with Docker service. Redis, Kafka, Elasticsearch containers are also available.

Spring Boot OpenAPI documentation with Springdoc: dependency `springdoc-openapi-starter-webmvc-ui`. Auto-generates OpenAPI 3 spec from Spring MVC controllers. Access at `/swagger-ui.html` and `/v3/api-docs`. Customize: `@Operation(summary = "Get user", tags = {"users"})` on controller methods. `@Parameter(description = "User ID")` on parameters. `@ApiResponse(responseCode = "404", description = "User not found")`. `@Schema(description = "User email", example = "user@example.com")` on model fields. Configure: `springdoc.api-docs.path=/api-docs; springdoc.swagger-ui.path=/swagger`. UNSAID: disable Swagger UI in production — it exposes your API surface to attackers. Use `springdoc.swagger-ui.enabled=false` and `springdoc.api-docs.enabled=false` for production profiles. For multi-module projects: use `@RouterOperation` for functional endpoints. Group APIs: `@Bean GroupedOpenApi adminApi() { return GroupedOpenApi.builder().group("admin").pathsToMatch("/admin/**").build(); }`. Springdoc supports both `spring-webmvc` and `spring-webflux` (reactive). Security schemes: `@SecurityScheme(name="Bearer", type=SecuritySchemeType.HTTP, scheme="bearer", bearerFormat="JWT")`.

Spring Boot Flyway database migration: dependency `spring-boot-starter-flyway`. Place SQL migrations in `src/main/resources/db/migration/` named `V1__create_users.sql`, `V2__add_email_index.sql`. Flyway runs automatically on startup, applying pending migrations in order. `spring.flyway.enabled=true; spring.flyway.locations=classpath:db/migration`. Custom migration with Java: implement `SpringJdbcMigration` for data migrations. UNSAID: Flyway uses a `flyway_schema_history` table to track applied migrations — never modify applied migration files (checksums are validated). For backward-compatible rolling deploys: (1) add column as nullable, (2) deploy app that writes both old and new columns, (3) backfill data, (4) add NOT NULL constraint, (5) remove old column reference in next deploy. `spring.flyway.out-of-order=false` (default) prevents running a lower-version migration after a higher one is applied — important for team development. For production: set `spring.flyway.validate-on-migrate=true` and `spring.flyway.clean-disabled=true` (prevents accidental `flyway:clean` which drops all tables). Liquibase vs Flyway: Flyway uses SQL (familiar, portable); Liquibase uses XML/YAML/JSON (more portable, supports rollback scripts).

Spring WebFlux reactive programming with Project Reactor: `Mono<T>` for 0-1 items, `Flux<T>` for 0-N items. Controller: `@GetMapping("/users/{id") Mono<User> getUser(@PathVariable Long id) { return userService.findById(id); }`. Compose: `userService.findById(id).flatMap(u -> orderService.findByUserId(u.getId()).collectList().map(orders -> new UserWithOrders(u, orders)))`. Error handling: `.onErrorReturn(fallback)`, `.onErrorResume(e -> Mono.just(fallback))`. Backpressure: `Flux.range(1, 1000).limitRate(100)`. UNSAID: WebFlux is event-loop based (Netty), not thread-per-request — excellent for high-concurrency I/O-bound workloads. Never block inside a reactive chain: `Mono.fromCallable(() -> blockingCall()).subscribeOn(Schedulers.boundedElastic())` offloads blocking work. `blockingGet()` or `.block()` in production reactive code causes event loop starvation. Spring Boot 3.2+ with virtual threads (`spring.threads.virtual.enabled=true`) on Spring MVC provides similar throughput to WebFlux without reactive complexity — prefer virtual threads + MVC unless you need streaming/backpressure. R2DBC for reactive database access (replaces JDBC in reactive stacks). `WebClient` replaces `RestTemplate` for reactive HTTP clients.

FAILURE: Spring Boot `@Value` injection in non-Spring-managed classes and in constructors before context initialization. `@Value` annotation works only in Spring-managed beans (classes created by the Spring IoC container). Using `@Value` in a plain Java class (not `@Component` or `@Bean`) results in null. Also, referencing a `@Value`-injected field in an `@PostConstruct` method works correctly, but using it in the constructor itself may fail if the field injection hasn't happened yet. Fix: use constructor injection: `public MyService(@Value("${app.timeout}") int timeout) { this.timeout = timeout; }`. UNSAID: constructor injection is always preferable to field injection for `@Value` and `@Autowired` — it makes dependencies explicit, enables testing without Spring context (just call the constructor), and prevents NPE from injection timing. Field injection (`@Autowired` on fields) is only possible after the constructor runs — `@PostConstruct` is guaranteed to run after all injections complete. For static fields: `@Value` does not work on static fields — use `@Bean` factory method or `@PostConstruct` to assign to static variable from instance variable. Spring's `Environment` interface provides programmatic property access without `@Value`.

Spring Boot Jackson ObjectMapper customization: `@Bean public ObjectMapper objectMapper() { return JsonMapper.builder().addModule(new JavaTimeModule()).disable(SerializationFeature.WRITE_DATES_AS_TIMESTAMPS).disable(DeserializationFeature.FAIL_ON_UNKNOWN_PROPERTIES).enable(MapperFeature.ACCEPT_CASE_INSENSITIVE_ENUMS).serializationInclusion(Include.NON_NULL).build(); }`. For custom serializer: `@JsonComponent class UserSerializer extends JsonSerializer<User>`. For `Instant`/`LocalDateTime`: `JavaTimeModule` with `ISO-8601` strings (not timestamps). UNSAID: always disable `FAIL_ON_UNKNOWN_PROPERTIES` for API clients — forward compatibility requires ignoring unknown fields. Enable `NON_NULL` serialization to reduce response payload size (omit null fields). For enums: `ACCEPT_CASE_INSENSITIVE_ENUMS` prevents 400 errors from case mismatches. `WRITE_DATES_AS_TIMESTAMPS=false` produces ISO-8601 strings for all `java.time` types. Register `ObjectMapper` as a Spring bean and it's automatically used by Spring MVC. For performance-sensitive APIs: `afterburner` module (`jackson-module-afterburner`) generates bytecode for faster serialization. `@JsonView` for filtered serialization (public vs admin response shapes). Never use `ObjectMapper.readValue(json, Object.class)` — returns `LinkedHashMap`, not your type.

Spring Boot WebClient for non-blocking HTTP calls: `@Bean WebClient webClient(WebClient.Builder builder) { return builder.baseUrl("https://api.external.com").defaultHeader("X-API-Key", apiKey).codecs(c -> c.defaultCodecs().maxInMemorySize(2 * 1024 * 1024)).build(); }`. Make request: `webClient.get().uri("/users/{id}", id).retrieve().onStatus(HttpStatusCode::isError, resp -> resp.bodyToMono(String.class).map(RuntimeException::new)).bodyToMono(User.class)`. With timeout: `.retrieve().bodyToMono(User.class).timeout(Duration.ofSeconds(5))`. UNSAID: `WebClient` replaced `RestTemplate` in Spring 5 — `RestTemplate` is in maintenance mode. For Spring MVC (non-reactive), use `WebClient` for external HTTP calls: it is still usable in MVC, just `.block()` at the boundary (or use virtual threads). `RestClient` (Spring 6.1+) is a synchronous alternative with a modern fluent API: `RestClient.create().get().uri(url).retrieve().body(User.class)` — simpler than WebClient for synchronous use cases. Retry with `WebClient`: `.retryWhen(Retry.backoff(3, Duration.ofMillis(100)).filter(e -> e instanceof WebClientRequestException))`. Connection pool: configure `ReactorClientHttpConnector` with `HttpClient` for pool sizing.

Spring Boot Bean Validation (JSR-380) with `@Valid` and custom constraints: annotate request body: `@PostMapping void create(@Valid @RequestBody UserRequest req)`. Custom constraint: `@Constraint(validatedBy = UniqueEmailValidator.class) @Target(FIELD) @Retention(RUNTIME) @interface UniqueEmail { String message() default "Email already registered"; }`. Validator: `class UniqueEmailValidator implements ConstraintValidator<UniqueEmail, String> { public boolean isValid(String email, ConstraintValidatorContext ctx) { return !userRepository.existsByEmail(email); } }`. Groups: `@Validated(CreateGroup.class)` for group-specific validation. UNSAID: `@Valid` triggers validation recursively on nested objects; `@Validated` (Spring) supports validation groups. `@Valid` on `@PathVariable` and `@RequestParam` requires `@Validated` on the controller class (not just the parameter). Constraint injection: Spring-managed validators can `@Autowired` beans (e.g., `userRepository`). Cross-field validation: implement `@Constraint` on the class level. For asymmetric request/response DTOs: separate `@Valid` request DTOs from response DTOs — never reuse the same class for both (different validation rules). `ConstraintValidator.initialize()` is called once at startup — use it to validate constraint configuration.

Spring Boot `@EventListener` for domain events: publish: `applicationEventPublisher.publishEvent(new UserCreatedEvent(user))`. Listen: `@EventListener(UserCreatedEvent.class) void onUserCreated(UserCreatedEvent event) { emailService.sendWelcome(event.getUser()); }`. Async listener: `@Async @EventListener`. Transactional listener (runs after commit): `@TransactionalEventListener(phase = TransactionPhase.AFTER_COMMIT)`. UNSAID: `@TransactionalEventListener` with `AFTER_COMMIT` is crucial for events that trigger side effects (emails, notifications) — ensures the database commit succeeded before sending. If the transaction rolls back, the event listener does not run. Default `@EventListener` runs within the same transaction as the publisher — useful for transactional side effects but risks rollback due to listener failures. For decoupled event-driven architecture: use a message broker (Kafka, RabbitMQ) via Spring Integration or Spring Cloud Stream for events that should survive application restarts. For within-JVM events that need ordering guarantees: `@Order` on listeners. Spring Framework's `ApplicationEvent` can carry any payload — no need to extend `ApplicationEvent` since Spring 4.2 (use plain POJOs as events).

Spring AOP `@Aspect` for cross-cutting concerns: `@Aspect @Component public class AuditAspect { @Around("@annotation(Audited)") public Object audit(ProceedingJoinPoint pjp) throws Throwable { String method = pjp.getSignature().toShortString(); Object[] args = pjp.getArgs(); log.info("Calling {} with {}", method, args); Object result = pjp.proceed(); log.info("{} returned {}", method, result); return result; } }`. Pointcut expressions: `execution(* com.example.service.*.*(..))` — all methods in service package. `@annotation(Transactional)` — methods with `@Transactional`. `within(@Service *)` — all beans annotated `@Service`. UNSAID: Spring AOP uses proxies — advice only intercepts calls through the proxy (external calls), not self-invocations (same class). AspectJ (compile-time or load-time weaving) handles all call sites. `@Before`, `@After`, `@AfterReturning`, `@AfterThrowing` for non-wrapping aspects. `@Around` with `pjp.proceed()` enables value modification. Aspects are ordered with `@Order` — lower number runs first (outermost). `@EnableAspectJAutoProxy(proxyTargetClass=true)` forces CGLIB proxying for classes without interfaces. Performance: each proxied method call adds overhead — avoid fine-grained AOP on hot paths.

FAILURE: Spring Boot profile activation confusion between `spring.profiles.active` and `spring.config.activate.on-profile`. `spring.profiles.active=prod` activates the `prod` profile and loads `application-prod.yml`. But placing `spring.profiles.active=prod` inside `application-prod.yml` causes a warning and is ignored in newer Spring Boot versions. For conditional config file loading: use `spring.config.activate.on-profile=prod` inside a config file section. For programmatic profile addition: `ConfigurableEnvironment.addActiveProfile()`. UNSAID: Spring Boot 2.4+ changed profile loading behavior — `spring.profiles` is deprecated in favor of `spring.config.activate.on-profile`. Profile-specific files (`application-{profile}.yml`) are loaded automatically when the profile is active — no need for explicit activation inside them. Common mistake: trying to set `spring.profiles.active` in application-prod.yml to force other profiles — this doesn't work. Correct pattern: `SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE=prod,aws` environment variable to activate multiple profiles in production. Testing: `@ActiveProfiles("test")` on test class, or `@TestPropertySource(properties = "spring.profiles.active=test")`.

Spring Boot method-level security with `@PreAuthorize`: `@EnableMethodSecurity` on `@Configuration`. Use: `@PreAuthorize("hasRole('ADMIN')")`, `@PreAuthorize("hasAuthority('USER_READ')")`, `@PreAuthorize("#userId == authentication.principal.id")` (SpEL for owner check). `@PostAuthorize("returnObject.ownerId == authentication.principal.id")` — filters return value. `@PreFilter` and `@PostFilter` for collections. UNSAID: `@EnableMethodSecurity` replaces `@EnableGlobalMethodSecurity` in Spring Security 6 — the old annotation is deprecated. `@Secured` is a simpler alternative that only accepts role names without SpEL. SpEL access to principal: `authentication.principal` returns the `UserDetails` object — cast to your custom `UserDetails` implementation to access custom fields. Test security: `@WithMockUser(roles="ADMIN")` from `spring-security-test`. `@WithSecurityContext` for complex custom principal setup. Method security adds AOP overhead — avoid for public methods or high-throughput internal calls. Authorization logic in SpEL can be complex to test — consider extracting to a `@Component` and calling it: `@PreAuthorize("@authService.canAccess(#id, authentication)")`

Spring Boot CORS configuration: global: `@Bean WebMvcConfigurer corsConfigurer() { return new WebMvcConfigurer() { @Override public void addCorsMappings(CorsRegistry registry) { registry.addMapping("/api/**").allowedOrigins("https://app.example.com").allowedMethods("GET","POST","PUT","DELETE").allowedHeaders("*").allowCredentials(true).maxAge(3600); } }; }`. Per-controller: `@CrossOrigin(origins = "https://app.example.com")`. With Spring Security: `http.cors(Customizer.withDefaults())` + define `CorsConfigurationSource` bean. UNSAID: Spring Security processes requests before Spring MVC — CORS preflight (`OPTIONS`) must be explicitly allowed in Spring Security, otherwise it returns 401 before reaching the CORS configuration. `http.cors(Customizer.withDefaults())` uses a `CorsConfigurationSource` bean if defined, otherwise falls back to `@CrossOrigin` annotations. Never use `allowedOrigins("*")` with `allowCredentials(true)` — browsers reject this (CORS spec violation). For development: use `allowedOriginPatterns("http://localhost:*")`. Misonfiguration: if CORS is configured in both Spring Security and Spring MVC, Security's config takes precedence.

Spring Boot GraalVM Native Image: compile to a native executable with `./mvnw -Pnative native:compile` or `./gradlew nativeCompile`. Spring Boot 3 includes AOT (Ahead-Of-Time) processing — generates hints for reflection, resources, and proxies. Runtime performance: startup in <100ms, much lower memory than JVM. For libraries without GraalVM support: add `RuntimeHintsRegistrar`: `@ImportRuntimeHints(MyHints.class) class MyConfig { static class MyHints implements RuntimeHintsRegistrar { public void registerHints(RuntimeHints hints, ClassLoader cl) { hints.reflection().registerType(MyClass.class, INVOKE_PUBLIC_METHODS); } } }`. UNSAID: GraalVM Native Image performs static analysis at compile time — dynamic class loading, reflection without hints, and runtime proxy generation fail silently or at startup. Test native image compilation in CI: `./mvnw -Pnative test` runs tests against the native executable. Many Spring Boot starters are native-compatible out of the box (Spring Data, Security, WebMVC). Incompatible libraries: anything using heavy runtime reflection (some older Hibernate versions, dynamic scripting). JVM startup time (100-500ms) is often acceptable — prefer native only if startup time is a hard requirement (serverless, rapid scaling).

Spring Boot structured logging with Logback and Logstash encoder: dependency `logstash-logback-encoder`. `logback-spring.xml`: `<appender name="JSON" class="ch.qos.logback.core.ConsoleAppender"><encoder class="net.logstash.logback.encoder.LogstashEncoder"><includeCallerData>false</includeCallerData></encoder></appender>`. Add MDC context for request tracing: `MDC.put("requestId", requestId); try { doWork(); } finally { MDC.remove("requestId"); }`. Spring Boot 3.4+ provides JSON logging without Logstash: `logging.structured.format.console=ecs`. UNSAID: `includeCallerData=false` (don't include file/line — expensive). MDC (Mapped Diagnostic Context) propagates to all log statements in the current thread — ideal for request-scoped fields like user ID, request ID, trace ID. For async/virtual threads: MDC is thread-local and does not automatically propagate to spawned threads — use `MDCAdapter` or copy MDC at thread boundary. Logback `spring.profiles` configuration: `<springProfile name="prod"><root level="WARN">` — different log levels per profile. `logging.level.com.example=DEBUG` in `application.yml` for package-level control. Avoid logging binary data, PII, passwords — scrub before logging.

FAILURE: Spring Boot `@Autowired` field injection in unit tests without Spring context. When testing a class with `@Autowired` fields using plain JUnit5 (no `@SpringBootTest`), the injected fields are null — Spring context is not started, so injection does not occur. Attempting `new MyService()` directly leaves all `@Autowired` fields as null. Fix: use constructor injection in production code: `public MyService(UserRepository repo) { this.repo = repo; }`. In tests: `new MyService(mockRepo)`. Or use `@ExtendWith(MockitoExtension.class)` with `@InjectMocks MyService service` and `@Mock UserRepository repo`. UNSAID: `@InjectMocks` uses Mockito's injection strategy: constructor injection first (preferred), then setter injection, then field injection — it does NOT start Spring. This is why constructor injection is the superior pattern: it enables testing without any framework magic. `@SpringBootTest` starts the entire application context — use only for integration tests, not unit tests. `@WebMvcTest`, `@DataJpaTest`, `@JsonTest` are slice tests that start minimal partial contexts. For pure unit tests: no Spring annotations in test class, plain constructor injection, Mockito for mocks.

Spring Boot `@WebMvcTest` for controller unit tests: `@WebMvcTest(UserController.class) class UserControllerTest { @Autowired MockMvc mvc; @MockBean UserService userService; @Test void getUser() throws Exception { given(userService.findById(1L)).willReturn(new User(1L, "Alice")); mvc.perform(get("/api/users/1").header("Authorization", "Bearer token")).andExpect(status().isOk()).andExpect(jsonPath("$.name").value("Alice")); } }`. UNSAID: `@WebMvcTest` loads only the web layer (controllers, filters, `@ControllerAdvice`) — not `@Service` or `@Repository` beans. All service dependencies must be provided via `@MockBean`. Security: `@WebMvcTest` auto-configures Spring Security — use `@WithMockUser` or disable security with `@AutoConfigureMockMvc(addFilters=false)` for simpler tests. `@MockBean` creates a Mockito mock and registers it as a Spring bean — different from `@Mock` (Mockito only, no Spring context). For JSON assertions: `jsonPath` with Hamcrest matchers or `json().isEqualTo()` with `@AutoConfigureJsonTesters`. For file upload: `MockMvcRequestBuilders.multipart()`. `MockMvc` does not start a real HTTP server — use `@SpringBootTest(webEnvironment=RANDOM_PORT)` + `TestRestTemplate` or `WebTestClient` for full integration tests.

Spring Boot R2DBC for reactive database access: dependency `spring-boot-starter-data-r2dbc` + driver (e.g., `r2dbc-postgresql`). Config: `spring.r2dbc.url=r2dbc:postgresql://localhost/dbname`. Repository extends `ReactiveCrudRepository<User, Long>`: methods return `Mono<User>` and `Flux<User>`. Custom query: `@Query("SELECT * FROM users WHERE email = :email") Mono<User> findByEmail(String email)`. `DatabaseClient` for complex queries: `databaseClient.sql("SELECT ...").fetch().all()`. UNSAID: R2DBC is the reactive alternative to JDBC — use it only if your stack is fully reactive (WebFlux). Mixing R2DBC in a Tomcat/servlet stack negates the benefit. Unlike JPA, R2DBC has no first-class relationship mapping (`@OneToMany` etc.) — join results must be mapped manually or with `@R2dbcCustomConversions`. Transactions: `@Transactional` works with R2DBC via `ReactiveTransactionManager`. For complex aggregates: `DatabaseClient` with explicit JOIN queries and result mapping is more practical than trying to simulate ORM-style lazy loading in a reactive context. `spring.r2dbc.pool.max-size=20` for connection pool sizing (r2dbc-pool).

Spring Boot Spring Batch for bulk data processing: define a `Job` with `Step`s: `@Bean Job importJob(JobRepository repo, Step importStep) { return new JobBuilder("import", repo).start(importStep).build(); }`. Chunk-oriented step: `@Bean Step importStep(JobRepository repo, PlatformTransactionManager tm) { return new StepBuilder("import", repo).<InputRecord, OutputRecord>chunk(100, tm).reader(fileReader()).processor(recordProcessor()).writer(dbWriter()).build(); }`. Run: `jobLauncher.run(job, new JobParameters())`. UNSAID: chunk-oriented processing reads N items, processes each, then writes the batch atomically in a transaction. On failure, only the current chunk is rolled back — not the entire job. `JobRepository` tracks execution status in a database table — restart picks up from the last failed chunk. `@StepScope` allows late binding of `@Value("#{jobParameters['file']}")` in readers/writers. For scheduled batch jobs: combine with `@Scheduled` + ShedLock. Parallel step execution: `new FlowBuilder<>(...).split(executor).add(flow1, flow2).end()`. Partitioning for large datasets: `PartitionStep` divides data into ranges, processes in parallel threads. Avoid batch processing inside web request handlers — use async job submission with progress tracking.

Spring Cloud Stream for Kafka/RabbitMQ messaging: `@Bean Consumer<UserCreatedEvent> handleUserCreated() { return event -> { emailService.sendWelcome(event); }; }`. `@Bean Supplier<Flux<OrderEvent>> orderEvents() { return () -> orderEventFlux; }`. `@Bean Function<UserCreatedEvent, WelcomeEmailCommand> processUser() { return event -> new WelcomeEmailCommand(event.getEmail()); }`. Configure: `spring.cloud.stream.bindings.handleUserCreated-in-0.destination=user-created; group=email-service`. UNSAID: Spring Cloud Stream abstracts the underlying message broker — same code works with Kafka and RabbitMQ by changing binder dependency. Functional model (since 3.x) replaces the older `@StreamListener`. Consumer group: `group=email-service` ensures only one instance in the group processes each message (Kafka consumer group semantics). For dead letter queues: `spring.cloud.stream.bindings.*.consumer.dead-letter-queue-name`. `@Bean Consumer<Flux<T>>` for reactive batch processing. Error handling: `spring.cloud.stream.bindings.*.consumer.max-attempts=3; backoff-initial-interval=1000` for retry with backoff. For transactional outbox pattern: publish events only after database transaction commits using `@TransactionalEventListener` + a `@KafkaListener` relay.

FAILURE: Spring Boot `@Transactional` with wrong propagation causing unexpected transaction nesting. `Propagation.REQUIRES_NEW` creates a new transaction, suspending the outer one — changes are committed independently. If an outer method with `@Transactional` calls an inner method with `@Transactional(propagation=REQUIRES_NEW)`, the inner transaction commits even if the outer rolls back. Symptoms: partial data committed when an operation that should be atomic has some parts committed and some rolled back. Fix: use the default `Propagation.REQUIRED` for most cases (joins the existing transaction). Use `REQUIRES_NEW` only for audit logging, metrics recording, or other operations that must persist even if the business transaction fails. UNSAID: `Propagation.MANDATORY` requires an existing transaction — throws `IllegalTransactionStateException` if no transaction is active (good for ensuring repository methods are always called in a transaction). `Propagation.NEVER` throws if a transaction is active — useful for read-only reporting that should not accidentally be part of a business transaction. The default `Propagation.REQUIRED` and `Isolation.DEFAULT` (database default, usually READ_COMMITTED for PostgreSQL) are correct for most business operations.

Spring Boot OpenTelemetry distributed tracing: dependency `spring-boot-starter-actuator` + `micrometer-tracing-bridge-otel` + `opentelemetry-exporter-otlp`. Auto-config with Spring Boot 3.x: `management.tracing.sampling.probability=1.0` (100% sampling, reduce in production). Export to Jaeger/Zipkin/OTLP collector. Trace context propagates automatically through RestTemplate, WebClient, `@Async`, Spring Cloud Stream. For custom spans: `@Autowired Tracer tracer; var span = tracer.nextSpan().name("operation").start(); try (var ws = tracer.withSpan(span)) { doWork(); } finally { span.end(); }`. UNSAID: `management.tracing.sampling.probability=0.1` (10%) is a common production setting — 100% sampling at high traffic is expensive. Use head-based sampling for distributed traces (Jaeger adaptive sampling, Tempo). Spring Boot 3 uses Micrometer Tracing as the abstraction layer — switch between OpenTelemetry and Brave backends without code changes. MDC bridge: `logging.pattern.level=%5p [${spring.application.name:},%X{traceId:-},%X{spanId:-}]` adds trace/span IDs to all log lines. For baggage propagation (user ID across services): `Baggage.current().getEntry("userId")`.

Spring Boot `@Conditional` annotations for feature flags and environment-specific beans: `@ConditionalOnProperty(name="feature.new-payment", havingValue="true", matchIfMissing=false)` — registers bean only if property is set. `@ConditionalOnMissingBean(PaymentService.class)` — registers default implementation only if no other bean of that type exists. `@ConditionalOnExpression("'${app.region}'.equals('eu')")`. `@Profile("!test")` — excludes from test profile. Custom: `@Conditional(MyCondition.class)` with `Condition` interface. UNSAID: `@ConditionalOnMissingBean` is used by Spring Boot auto-configuration to provide defaults that are overridable — if you define your own `DataSource` bean, the auto-configured one is not created. `@ConditionalOnProperty` is the standard feature flag mechanism without an external system. For more sophisticated feature flags: `FF4J`, `Unleash`, or LaunchDarkly Spring integration. `@Profile` is simpler than `@ConditionalOnProperty` for environment-based selection — prefer `@Profile` for environment differences, `@ConditionalOnProperty` for runtime feature toggles. `@ImportAutoConfiguration` for testing auto-configuration in isolation.

Spring Data Redis with `StringRedisTemplate` and `RedisTemplate`: `stringTemplate.opsForValue().set(key, value, Duration.ofMinutes(30))`. Hash: `stringTemplate.opsForHash().put(hashKey, field, value)`. List/Queue: `stringTemplate.opsForList().rightPush(key, value)`. Pipeline (batch): `stringTemplate.executePipelined(conn -> { ... })`. Pub/Sub: `@Bean MessageListenerAdapter listenerAdapter(ReceiverService receiver) { return new MessageListenerAdapter(receiver, "onMessage"); }`. UNSAID: `StringRedisTemplate` uses String serializers for both key and value — appropriate for most uses. `RedisTemplate<String, Object>` with `Jackson2JsonRedisSerializer` for typed objects — configure serializer to avoid ClassCastException: set `valueSerializer = new GenericJackson2JsonRedisSerializer()`. Key design: use namespaced keys `app:users:{id}` to avoid collisions and enable key scanning. `SCAN` instead of `KEYS *` for production key iteration — `KEYS *` blocks Redis. `TTL` is the most important cache correctness tool — always set TTLs to prevent stale data from living forever. For distributed locks: `SET key value NX PX 30000` (atomic set-if-not-exists with expiry) via `stringTemplate.opsForValue().setIfAbsent(key, value, Duration.ofSeconds(30))`.

FAILURE: Spring Boot `@Transactional(readOnly=true)` not preventing writes and causing unexpected behavior. `readOnly=true` is a hint — it does NOT prevent write operations at the Java level. It signals to the underlying transaction manager and database that no writes are expected, enabling optimizations (MySQL: uses read replica; Hibernate: skips dirty-checking; JPA: skips flush on commit). Misuse: marking a service method `readOnly=true` that actually performs writes — the write still happens in PostgreSQL, but JPA/Hibernate may not flush changes, causing silent data loss. Also: calling a `readOnly=true` @Transactional method from a write transaction — Spring joins the existing transaction but the `readOnly` hint is ignored in some implementations. UNSAID: `readOnly=true` with Hibernate prevents automatic flushing of the first-level cache — changes to managed entities are not persisted. This is useful for read operations where you manipulate entities without wanting writes. For Spring Data JPA: mark all query repository methods with `@Transactional(readOnly=true)` by default, override with `@Transactional` for write methods. Read replicas: `@Transactional(readOnly=true)` combined with Spring's `AbstractRoutingDataSource` to route to read replicas.

Spring Boot Micrometer metrics for custom instrumentation: `@Autowired MeterRegistry registry`. Counter: `registry.counter("orders.created", "status", status.name()).increment()`. Timer: `Timer.Sample sample = Timer.start(registry); processOrder(); sample.stop(registry.timer("orders.processing.time"))`. Gauge: `registry.gauge("queue.size", queue, ArrayDeque::size)`. Distribution summary: `registry.summary("request.size", "endpoint", endpoint).record(requestSize)`. Use `@Timed("method.name")` on methods with `TimedAspect` bean. UNSAID: high-cardinality tags (user ID, request ID per metric) create millions of time series — Prometheus, Graphite, and most TSDBs will OOM or drop data. Keep tag cardinality low (< ~100 values per tag). `registry.getMeters()` for inspecting registered meters. `MeterBinder` for reusable metric registration (library pattern). Spring Boot auto-registers JVM metrics (`jvm.memory.used`, `jvm.gc.pause`), Tomcat metrics, HikariCP metrics, and cache metrics. For SLO tracking: use `Timer.builder(...).publishPercentiles(0.5, 0.95, 0.99)` for Prometheus histograms. `management.metrics.distribution.percentiles-histogram.*=true` for Micrometer histograms compatible with Prometheus.

Spring Boot `@DataJpaTest` for repository layer tests: `@DataJpaTest class UserRepositoryTest { @Autowired UserRepository repo; @Autowired TestEntityManager em; @Test void findByEmail() { em.persist(new User("alice@example.com", "Alice")); em.flush(); User found = repo.findByEmail("alice@example.com"); assertThat(found.getName()).isEqualTo("Alice"); } }`. UNSAID: `@DataJpaTest` configures an in-memory H2 database by default, rolls back after each test (`@Transactional`), and loads only JPA-related beans. To use the real database (PostgreSQL with Testcontainers): `@AutoConfigureTestDatabase(replace=Replace.NONE)` on the test class + Testcontainers configuration. H2 compatibility issues: H2 does not support all PostgreSQL features (JSON columns, PostGIS, certain functions) — use real database for tests involving these. `TestEntityManager` wraps `EntityManager` with testing helpers: `persist`, `find`, `flush`, `clear`. `@Rollback(false)` to commit test data (useful for manual inspection but can pollute test isolation). `@Sql("/schema.sql")` for pre-loading test data. For complex repository queries: always test against a real database — H2 query planner behavior differs.

Spring Boot multipart file upload handling: controller: `@PostMapping("/upload") ResponseEntity<String> upload(@RequestParam MultipartFile file) { if (file.isEmpty()) return bad; String filename = StringUtils.cleanPath(file.getOriginalFilename()); Path target = uploadDir.resolve(filename); Files.copy(file.getInputStream(), target, REPLACE_EXISTING); return ok(filename); }`. Configure limits: `spring.servlet.multipart.max-file-size=10MB; spring.servlet.multipart.max-request-size=10MB`. S3 upload: `s3Client.putObject(PutObjectRequest.builder().bucket(bucket).key(filename).build(), RequestBody.fromInputStream(file.getInputStream(), file.getSize()))`. UNSAID: always sanitize the filename — `StringUtils.cleanPath` removes `../` path traversal attacks. Never use `getOriginalFilename()` directly as a filesystem path. Validate file type by MIME type and magic bytes, not just extension: `Tika.detect(file.getInputStream())` for content-type detection. For large files: use `MultipartFile.transferTo(path)` to avoid buffering in memory. `spring.servlet.multipart.file-size-threshold=2KB` — files smaller than this are buffered in memory, larger written to disk temp. In production: upload directly to cloud storage (S3/GCS) using presigned URLs — avoid routing binary data through the application server.

Spring Boot `@RestTemplate` vs `RestClient` (Spring 6.1+) for synchronous HTTP: `RestClient` has a fluent builder API: `RestClient client = RestClient.builder().baseUrl("https://api.example.com").defaultHeader("Authorization", "Bearer " + token).build()`. Request: `User user = client.get().uri("/users/{id}", id).retrieve().onStatus(HttpStatusCode::is4xxClientError, (req, res) -> { throw new UserNotFoundException(id); }).body(User.class)`. POST: `client.post().uri("/users").body(new UserRequest("Alice")).retrieve().toBodilessEntity()`. UNSAID: `RestTemplate` is in maintenance mode since Spring 5 — use `RestClient` (Spring 6.1+) or `WebClient` for new code. `RestClient` is synchronous and blocking — appropriate for Spring MVC + virtual threads. `WebClient` is non-blocking — appropriate for WebFlux or when you need reactive composition. `RestClient.Builder` can be customized globally (interceptors, error handlers) and then cloned per-service: `clientBuilder.clone().baseUrl(differentUrl).build()`. For load-balanced calls in Spring Cloud: `@LoadBalanced RestClient.Builder` — integrates with service discovery (Eureka, Kubernetes).

Spring Boot Hibernate second-level cache with Caffeine: dependency `hibernate-jcache` + `caffeine`. Config: `spring.jpa.properties.hibernate.cache.use_second_level_cache=true; spring.jpa.properties.hibernate.cache.region.factory_class=jcache; spring.cache.caffeine.spec=maximumSize=500,expireAfterWrite=5m`. Entity: `@Entity @Cache(usage=CacheConcurrencyStrategy.READ_WRITE) public class Product`. UNSAID: second-level cache is shared across all sessions in the same JVM — stale data risk in distributed deployments (multiple instances). For multi-instance deployments: use Redis-backed second-level cache (`hibernate-redis-cache`) for cross-instance cache sharing. Cache concurrency strategies: `READ_ONLY` for immutable entities (best performance); `READ_WRITE` for mutable entities (safe, serialized access); `NONSTRICT_READ_WRITE` (risk of short stale windows); `TRANSACTIONAL` (JTA required). Query cache: `spring.jpa.properties.hibernate.cache.use_query_cache=true` + `@QueryHint(name=HINT_CACHEABLE, value="true")` on JPQL queries. Second-level cache hit/miss: `Hibernate.initialize(entity)` to force load, `EntityManager.getEntityManagerFactory().getCache().evict(Product.class, id)` to evict. Monitor cache statistics via JMX or Actuator metrics.

FAILURE: Spring Boot lazy initialization (`spring.main.lazy-initialization=true`) masking startup errors in production. Lazy initialization defers bean creation until the bean is first requested — startup is faster but errors in bean configuration (missing dependencies, misconfigured properties) are discovered only when the bean is first used, potentially in production under load. A broken `DataSource` configuration would not fail at startup — the application starts, then the first request that needs DB access fails. Fix: use lazy initialization only for development (faster restarts). In production: keep `lazy-initialization=false` (default) to fail fast on misconfiguration. UNSAID: targeted laziness is acceptable: `@Lazy` on specific expensive beans that are rarely used. For testing: `spring.main.lazy-initialization=true` combined with `@MockBean` for fast test startup that doesn't instantiate unused beans. `SpringApplication.setLazyInitialization(true)` sets it programmatically. For health checks: if lazy init is used, ensure the readiness probe exercises all critical beans before traffic is routed. Spring Boot 2.2+ introduced lazy initialization. Context refresh time vs first-request latency tradeoff — in Kubernetes with autoscaling, slow startup may cause timeout during rollout.

Spring Boot JPA auditing with `@CreatedDate`, `@LastModifiedDate`: enable with `@EnableJpaAuditing` on `@Configuration`. Base entity: `@MappedSuperclass @EntityListeners(AuditingEntityListener.class) public abstract class AuditableEntity { @CreatedDate @Column(updatable=false) protected Instant createdAt; @LastModifiedDate protected Instant updatedAt; @CreatedBy @Column(updatable=false) protected String createdBy; @LastModifiedBy protected String updatedBy; }`. For `@CreatedBy`/`@LastModifiedBy`: implement `AuditorAware<String>`: `@Bean AuditorAware<String> auditorProvider() { return () -> Optional.ofNullable(SecurityContextHolder.getContext().getAuthentication()).map(Authentication::getName); }`. UNSAID: `@Column(updatable=false)` on `@CreatedDate` prevents accidental overwrite on update. `@LastModifiedDate` is automatically updated by JPA on every save — no manual code needed. `Instant` is the best type for audit timestamps (UTC, no timezone ambiguity). For soft deletes: add `@Column(name="deleted_at") Instant deletedAt` and use `@Where(clause="deleted_at IS NULL")` on the entity for automatic filtering. `@SQLDelete(sql="UPDATE entity SET deleted_at=now() WHERE id=?")` for soft delete on `repository.delete()`.

Spring Boot integration with Apache Kafka using `spring-kafka`: producer: `kafkaTemplate.send("orders", orderId, new OrderCreatedEvent(orderId, amount))`. Consumer: `@KafkaListener(topics="orders", groupId="order-processor", containerFactory="kafkaListenerContainerFactory") void process(OrderCreatedEvent event, @Header(KafkaHeaders.RECEIVED_PARTITION) int partition)`. Error handling: `@KafkaListener ... @RetryableTopic(attempts="3", backoff=@Backoff(delay=1000, multiplier=2), autoCreateTopics=false)` for automatic retry topics. DLT (Dead Letter Topic) destination: `orders-dlt`. UNSAID: `@KafkaListener` methods run in a listener container thread pool — configure `concurrency` to match the number of partitions: `@KafkaListener(concurrency="${topic.partitions:3}")`. Message ordering: Kafka guarantees ordering within a partition — use a consistent partition key (e.g., entity ID) to ensure related events are processed in order. Idempotent consumers: Kafka at-least-once delivery means consumers may receive duplicates — implement idempotency using a `processed_events` table or Redis SET. `@RetryableTopic` creates retry topics (`orders-retry-1`, `orders-retry-2`) and DLT automatically. Transactional producer: `kafkaTemplate.executeInTransaction(t -> { t.send(...); return true; })` for exactly-once within Kafka.

Spring Boot `@Conditional` for auto-configuration and `spring.factories` / `AutoConfiguration.imports` for library authors: create auto-configuration class: `@AutoConfiguration @ConditionalOnClass(Foo.class) @ConditionalOnMissingBean(FooService.class) public class FooAutoConfiguration { @Bean FooService fooService(FooProperties props) { return new FooService(props); } }`. Register in `META-INF/spring/org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.AutoConfiguration.imports` (Spring Boot 3+): `com.example.FooAutoConfiguration`. UNSAID: Spring Boot 3 replaced `spring.factories` (`EnableAutoConfiguration`) with `AutoConfiguration.imports` file for better build performance (AOT processing). Library auto-configurations should be `@ConditionalOnMissingBean` on all beans they provide — ensuring user-defined beans take precedence. Ordering: `@AutoConfigureBefore(DataSourceAutoConfiguration.class)` and `@AutoConfigureAfter` for dependency ordering. `@ConditionalOnWebApplication(type=SERVLET)` for web-only beans. Test auto-configuration with `ApplicationContextRunner`: `new ApplicationContextRunner().withConfiguration(AutoConfigurations.of(FooAutoConfiguration.class)).run(ctx -> assertThat(ctx).hasSingleBean(FooService.class))`.

FAILURE: Hibernate `LazyInitializationException` accessing lazy-loaded collection outside persistence context. Accessing a `@OneToMany(fetch=LAZY)` collection after the `EntityManager` is closed (outside `@Transactional` boundary, in service or controller layer) throws `LazyInitializationException: could not initialize proxy - no Session`. Common patterns: loading entity in repository, returning it to controller, controller accessing `entity.getOrders()` — the persistence context is closed between repository and controller. Fix: (1) `JOIN FETCH` in the query; (2) `@EntityGraph`; (3) use DTO projection; (4) open-session-in-view pattern (anti-pattern for production). UNSAID: `spring.jpa.open-in-view=true` (Spring Boot default!) keeps the persistence context open for the entire HTTP request — convenient but causes N+1 queries in controllers, binds database resources to HTTP threads, and hides architectural problems. Set `spring.jpa.open-in-view=false` in production and fix the underlying design. The correct architecture: load all needed data in the service layer (within `@Transactional`), return DTOs (not entities) to the controller layer. This eliminates lazy loading in controllers by design. Hibernate `@BatchSize(size=30)` as a partial mitigation — reduces N+1 to N/30+1 queries.

Spring Boot production Docker containerization: multi-stage Dockerfile with Spring Boot layered jar: `FROM eclipse-temurin:21-jre AS build; WORKDIR /app; COPY target/*.jar app.jar; RUN java -Djarmode=layertools -jar app.jar extract`. Final stage: `FROM eclipse-temurin:21-jre; RUN useradd -r app; USER app; WORKDIR /app; COPY --from=build /app/dependencies ./; COPY --from=build /app/spring-boot-loader ./; COPY --from=build /app/snapshot-dependencies ./; COPY --from=build /app/application ./; ENTRYPOINT ["java","org.springframework.boot.loader.launch.JarLauncher"]`. JVM flags for containers: `JAVA_OPTS: -XX:MaxRAMPercentage=75 -XX:+UseContainerSupport`. UNSAID: `UseContainerSupport` (default in JDK 11+) reads cgroup memory limits instead of host RAM — prevents OOM in containers. `MaxRAMPercentage=75` leaves headroom for off-heap (Metaspace, threads, native memory). Spring Boot's layered jar separates dependencies from application code — Docker layer cache reuses the dependencies layer on code-only changes, significantly faster builds. Run as non-root (`useradd -r app`) for container security. JVM `+XX:+PrintFlagsFinal` to verify effective flags. For JDK 21+: consider virtual threads to reduce thread count and memory.

Spring Boot Specification API for dynamic queries: implement `Specification<T>`: `public static Specification<User> hasEmail(String email) { return (root, query, cb) -> email == null ? null : cb.equal(root.get("email"), email); }`. Combine: `Specification<User> spec = Specification.where(hasEmail(filter.email)).and(isActive(filter.active)).and(createdAfter(filter.startDate))`. Repository extends `JpaSpecificationExecutor<User>`. Call: `userRepository.findAll(spec, pageable)`. UNSAID: `null` predicates returned from `Specification` are handled correctly — `Specification.where(null)` produces an open predicate (no WHERE clause restriction). This enables dynamic filter composition without null-checking each condition in the calling code. For complex reports: JPQL named queries or Querydsl are often more readable. `QueryDSL` with `querydsl-jpa` generates Q-classes from entities: `QUser user = QUser.user; JPAQuery<User> query = queryFactory.selectFrom(user).where(user.email.eq(email).and(user.active.isTrue()))`. Querydsl provides type-safe dynamic query building with IDE autocompletion. For Spring Data: `QuerydslPredicateExecutor<T>` mixin enables `repository.findAll(predicate, pageable)`.

Spring Boot Liquibase migration: dependency `spring-boot-starter-data-jpa` + `liquibase-core`. Master changelog: `src/main/resources/db/changelog/db.changelog-master.yaml`. Include changelogs: `databaseChangeLog: - include: file: db/changelog/001-create-users.yaml relativeToChangelogFile: false`. Changeset: `changeSet: id: 001 author: alice changes: - createTable: tableName: users columns: - column: name: id type: BIGINT constraints: primaryKey: true`. UNSAID: Liquibase tracks applied changesets in `DATABASECHANGELOG` table by `id+author+filename` composite key — never rename or change applied changesets. Rollback: define `<rollback>` in each changeset for `liquibase:rollback`. `context` parameter for environment-specific changesets: `context: prod`. For Spring Boot: `spring.liquibase.contexts=dev` activates dev-context changesets. `liquibase.should.run=false` disables Liquibase (useful in test mode). Liquibase vs Flyway: Liquibase supports rollback scripts natively (Flyway Pro feature); Liquibase changelogs are XML/YAML (more verbose); Flyway uses SQL (simpler for DBA-friendly migrations). Both provide equivalent functionality for forward migrations.

Spring Boot `Records` and immutable DTOs (Java 14+): `public record UserResponse(Long id, String name, String email, Instant createdAt) {}`. Jackson serialization: works automatically with Jackson 2.12+ and `@JsonAutoDetect`. Deserialization: `@JsonCreator` or enable `jackson-module-parameter-names` (Spring Boot auto-configures). Validation: `public record CreateUserRequest(@NotBlank String name, @Email String email) {}`. Spring MVC `@RequestBody CreateUserRequest req` with `@Valid` — works with records. UNSAID: records are final, cannot be extended, and all fields are final — ideal for DTOs that should never be mutated. `equals`, `hashCode`, `toString` are generated based on all fields. For compact constructors: `public CreateUserRequest { Objects.requireNonNull(name); }` — validates in the constructor. Records cannot implement JPA entities (`@Entity`) because they are final and JPA requires non-final, proxiable classes.
