Records work with Spring Data projections: `interface UserSummary { Long getId(); String getName(); }` or record projection via Spring Data 3.0+. `@Builder` (Lombok) is not needed for records — use `new UserResponse(id, name, email, createdAt)` directly.

FAILURE: Spring Security CSRF protection disabled globally when only API endpoints need exemption. `http.csrf(AbstractHttpConfigurer::disable)` disables CSRF for all endpoints — including form-based login if present. This enables CSRF attacks on any state-changing endpoint accessible from a browser session. Fix: for REST APIs using JWT (stateless): CSRF is not needed (cookies not used for auth) — disabling is correct for the API portion. For mixed apps (API + form-based auth): `http.csrf(csrf -> csrf.ignoringRequestMatchers("/api/**"))` — disable only for API paths. UNSAID: CSRF protection is only needed when using cookie-based session authentication (JSESSIONID) — if authentication uses `Authorization: Bearer <token>` header, CSRF is not exploitable (cross-origin requests cannot set custom headers). Disabling CSRF is safe for stateless JWT APIs. The mistake is in mixed apps where disabling all CSRF leaves the form-based login endpoint unprotected. Spring Security 6 default: CSRF protection is enabled. For SPA with same-origin backend: use `CookieCsrfTokenRepository.withHttpOnlyFalse()` so JavaScript can read the CSRF token from cookie.

Spring Boot `@Bean` factory methods and `@Configuration` class proxying: `@Configuration` classes use CGLIB proxying — `@Bean` method calls are intercepted and return the same singleton instance. `@Configuration(proxyBeanMethods=false)` disables proxying (LITE mode) — `@Bean` methods are plain factory methods, calling them again creates new instances. Use LITE mode when: beans are not called from other `@Bean` methods, and you want faster startup (no CGLIB). `@Component @Bean` combinations (LITE): when `@Bean` is in a `@Component` class, proxying is disabled automatically. UNSAID: `proxyBeanMethods=false` is used by many Spring Boot auto-configurations for performance. The common mistake: placing two `@Bean` methods that call each other in a `@Configuration(proxyBeanMethods=false)` class — the second call creates a new instance, not the singleton. Cyclic `@Bean` dependencies: `@Lazy` on one `@Autowired` constructor parameter to break the cycle — Spring defers injection of that bean. Better: redesign to remove the cycle. `@Bean` return types: use the most specific type that consumers should see — returning an interface hides implementation details.

Spring Boot JPA optimistic locking with `@Version`: add `@Version Long version` field to entity. On concurrent updates, Hibernate checks the version matches — if another transaction modified the entity, `OptimisticLockException` is thrown. Handle in service: `try { save(entity); } catch (OptimisticLockException e) { // reload and retry or return 409 Conflict }`. REST API: return `409 Conflict` with the current version. Client must include version in update request. UNSAID: optimistic locking is appropriate for low-contention scenarios — assumes conflicts are rare, reads are frequent, writes are less frequent. Pessimistic locking (`@Lock(LockModeType.PESSIMISTIC_WRITE)`) holds a database row lock for high-contention scenarios — serializes access but reduces throughput. `@Version` works with `@Entity` JPA entities — supports `Long`, `Integer`, `Timestamp` version types. `Timestamp` versions are less reliable than `Long` (clock skew issues). On `merge()` or `save()`, Hibernate generates `UPDATE ... WHERE id=? AND version=?` — zero rows updated triggers `OptimisticLockException`. After optimistic lock failure: reload the entity, reapply changes, retry — this merge-on-conflict pattern is idiomatic.

Spring Boot `@Retryable` with Spring Retry: dependency `spring-retry` + `spring-boot-starter-aop`. Enable: `@EnableRetry`. Annotate: `@Retryable(retryFor={TransientException.class}, maxAttempts=3, backoff=@Backoff(delay=1000, multiplier=2, maxDelay=10000)) CompletableFuture<Result> callExternal(Request req)`. Recovery: `@Recover Result recover(TransientException ex, Request req) { return fallback(); }`. UNSAID: `@Retryable` only retries on the declared exception types — other exceptions propagate immediately. Idempotency: only retry idempotent operations. `@CircuitBreaker` from Resilience4j is superior to `@Retryable` for external API calls — circuit breaker fast-fails when the remote is down, while `@Retryable` keeps trying until `maxAttempts`. Combine: `@CircuitBreaker @Retryable` — retry within the circuit breaker's window. `backoff.random = true` adds jitter to prevent retry storms. For HTTP clients: Resilience4j `@Retry` + `@TimeLimiter` together. Spring Retry's `RetryTemplate` for programmatic retry (when annotations are not feasible, e.g., lambda in stream). Retry context: `RetryContextHolder.getContext()` for per-attempt state.

Spring Boot gRPC server with `grpc-spring-boot-starter` (yidongnan/grpc-spring-boot-starter): annotate service implementation: `@GrpcService public class UserGrpcService extends UserServiceGrpc.UserServiceImplBase { @Override public void getUser(GetUserRequest req, StreamObserver<UserResponse> responseObserver) { User user = userService.findById(req.getId()); responseObserver.onNext(UserResponse.newBuilder().setId(user.getId()).setName(user.getName()).build()); responseObserver.onCompleted(); } }`. Config: `grpc.server.port=9090; grpc.server.security.enabled=true`. Client: `@GrpcClient("user-service") UserServiceGrpc.UserServiceBlockingStub userStub`. UNSAID: `grpc-spring-boot-starter` auto-configures gRPC server and integrates with Spring Security, Micrometer, and Spring Cloud discovery. Error handling: use `io.grpc.StatusRuntimeException` with gRPC status codes (`Status.NOT_FOUND`, `Status.INVALID_ARGUMENT`). Interceptors: `@GrpcGlobalServerInterceptor` for request logging, auth, tracing. For health: `grpc-health-probe` (Kubernetes native gRPC health check). `grpc.server.in-process-name` for testing without network. `grpc-netty-shaded` is the recommended transport (avoids Netty version conflicts).

Spring Boot `@SpEL` (Spring Expression Language) for complex conditions and configurations: in `@Cacheable`: `key="#user.id + ':' + #user.tenantId"`. In `@PreAuthorize`: `@PreAuthorize("#user.id == authentication.principal.id or hasRole('ADMIN')")`. In `@Value`: `@Value("#{systemProperties['user.home'] + '/config'}")`. In `@Conditional`: `@ConditionalOnExpression("'${app.mode}' == 'cluster' and ${app.nodes} > 1")`. SpEL supports: method invocations, property access, arithmetic, logical operators, ternary, regex (`matches`), collection projection (`{user.name | user.active }`). UNSAID: SpEL is powerful but difficult to test and debug — complex expressions should be moved to `@Component` beans with unit-testable methods and called from SpEL: `@PreAuthorize("@securityService.canAccess(#id)")`. SpEL is evaluated at runtime — type errors are runtime exceptions, not compile-time. In `@Cacheable`, `#result` is available in `condition` (post-invocation, not key). SpEL injection: never construct SpEL expressions from user input — SpEL can execute arbitrary Java code. `@Value` with SpEL is evaluated in the application context; `@Value("${prop}")` is property placeholder, different from `#{expression}`.

FAILURE: Spring Boot `@Async` exception handling silently swallowing errors on `void` return methods. `@Async` methods with `void` return type: uncaught exceptions are passed to `AsyncUncaughtExceptionHandler` — if none is configured, exceptions are logged at ERROR level and silently discarded. The caller has no way to know the async task failed. `@Async void sendEmail(String to)` — if `sendEmail` throws, the caller is never notified. Fix: return `CompletableFuture<Void>`: `@Async CompletableFuture<Void> sendEmail(String to) { try { emailClient.send(to); return CompletableFuture.completedFuture(null); } catch (Exception e) { return CompletableFuture.failedFuture(e); } }`. Caller: `sendEmail(to).exceptionally(e -> { log.error("Email failed", e); return null; })`. UNSAID: for fire-and-forget tasks where failure is acceptable (best-effort notifications): configure a custom `AsyncUncaughtExceptionHandler` via `AsyncConfigurer` to at least log failures to your monitoring system. For critical async tasks: always use `CompletableFuture` return to propagate errors. `@Async` with `void` return is appropriate only for truly optional background work where failure has no business consequence.

Spring Boot rate limiting with Bucket4j and Redis: dependency `bucket4j-spring-boot-starter` + `bucket4j-redis`. Configure via `application.yml`: `bucket4j.filters[0].url=.*; rate-limits[0].bandwidths[0].capacity=100; refill-tokens=100; refill-period=1m`. Per-user limiting: use a `KeyResolver` that extracts user ID from authentication. Programmatic: `Bucket bucket = Bucket.builder().addLimit(Bandwidth.classic(100, Refill.intervally(100, Duration.ofMinutes(1)))).build()`. Try consume: `if (!bucket.tryConsume(1)) { return ResponseEntity.status(429).header("X-Rate-Limit-Retry-After-Seconds", ...).build(); }`. UNSAID: Bucket4j supports multiple algorithms: classic token bucket (allows burst up to capacity), greedy (smooth rate). For distributed rate limiting (multiple instances): use Bucket4j + Redis backend — token counts are stored in Redis atomically via Lua scripts. Return `Retry-After` header per RFC 6585. For API gateway rate limiting: consider handling at infrastructure level (Kong, AWS API Gateway) rather than application level — simpler, no Redis dependency. Per-endpoint rate limiting: different limits for auth endpoints (strict: 10/min) vs read endpoints (relaxed: 1000/min).

Spring Boot `@DataJpaTest` with custom repositories and Specifications: create a `@TestConfiguration` in the test to provide required `@Bean`s not loaded by `@DataJpaTest`. For testing Specifications: create test data with `TestEntityManager.persist()`, then call `repository.findAll(specification)`. Test both positive matches and non-matches. Test ordering: `repository.findAll(spec, Sort.by("name").ascending())`. For native queries: `@DataJpaTest` with `@AutoConfigureTestDatabase(replace=Replace.NONE)` + Testcontainers for accurate query testing. UNSAID: `@DataJpaTest` auto-detects `@Entity` classes in the same package — if entities are in a different module, use `@EntityScan`. `@DataJpaTest` includes `spring-boot-test-autoconfigure` — it scans for `@Repository`, `@Entity`, `@Embeddable` components. Custom `@Component`s (service classes) are not loaded — use `@Import(MyCustomComponent.class)` if needed. For auditing tests: `@Import(JpaAuditingConfig.class)` to load `@EnableJpaAuditing` configuration. `TestEntityManager.flush()` followed by `TestEntityManager.clear()` forces JPA to write to DB and clear first-level cache — ensures subsequent reads hit the database (tests real persistence behavior).

Spring Boot `@ConditionalOnBean` and auto-wiring by type: Spring autowires by type by default — if multiple beans of the same type exist, use `@Qualifier("beanName")` or `@Primary`. `@Primary` marks the default bean when multiple exist. `@Qualifier` selects a specific bean by name. For collections: `@Autowired List<PaymentProcessor> processors` injects ALL beans of type `PaymentProcessor`. Order the list with `@Order(1)` on implementations. `@Primary` + `@Qualifier` together: `@Primary` for default injection; `@Qualifier` for explicit selection. UNSAID: `@ConditionalOnBean(DataSource.class)` — creates the bean only if a `DataSource` bean already exists. Vs `@ConditionalOnMissingBean`: present check vs absence check. Order dependency: `@ConditionalOnBean` depends on bean definition order — beans defined in `@Configuration` classes processed earlier may not be visible. Use `@DependsOn("otherBean")` to enforce ordering. For testing with multiple implementations: `@MockBean PaymentProcessor mockProcessor` replaces ALL beans of that type (not just the primary) — verify mock with `@Qualifier`. `@Autowired` collections are useful for strategy pattern implementations: collect all `Validator` beans and iterate.

Spring Boot `@Transactional` isolation levels and when to use them: `DEFAULT` (database default, PostgreSQL=READ_COMMITTED): appropriate for most operations. `REPEATABLE_READ`: same row returns same value within transaction — prevents non-repeatable reads. `SERIALIZABLE`: full isolation, no phantom reads — use for financial operations requiring strong consistency (inventory check-and-reserve, account balance operations). `READ_UNCOMMITTED`: reads uncommitted changes from other transactions — only for reporting where stale data is acceptable. Usage: `@Transactional(isolation=Isolation.SERIALIZABLE)`. UNSAID: `SERIALIZABLE` prevents phantom reads but causes `40001` serialization failure errors under concurrent load — implement retry logic. PostgreSQL implements `SERIALIZABLE` via Serializable Snapshot Isolation (SSI), more efficient than traditional locking. For distributed systems: database-level isolation is not sufficient — implement optimistic locking (`@Version`) or distributed locks for cross-service operations. `READ_COMMITTED` (default) allows non-repeatable reads — if your service reads a row twice in one transaction and another transaction modifies it in between, you get different values. For reporting transactions that span multiple tables: `REPEATABLE_READ` ensures consistent snapshot.

FAILURE: Spring Boot circular dependency injection causing application startup failure. Circular dependencies: `ServiceA` depends on `ServiceB`, `ServiceB` depends on `ServiceA` — Spring 6+ throws `BeanCurrentlyInCreationException` by default (Spring 5 allowed this via field injection + proxy). Fix: (1) Redesign — the most common cause is a missing third service that both depend on; (2) use `@Lazy` on one constructor parameter to break the cycle: `ServiceA(ServiceB b)` → `ServiceA(@Lazy ServiceB b)`. (3) Extract the shared logic to a new `ServiceC` that both `ServiceA` and `ServiceB` depend on. UNSAID: `spring.main.allow-circular-references=true` re-enables circular dependency resolution (Spring 5 behavior) — strongly discouraged as it patches the symptom not the cause. Circular dependencies often indicate a layering violation: Service A calls Service B's user-facing method, Service B calls Service A's user-facing method — this means they're not properly layered. The correct fix is almost always to extract shared logic. `@Lazy` on `@Autowired` field is the quickest fix but hides the design issue. Use `spring.main.allow-circular-references=false` (default in Spring Boot 3) as a guard.

Spring Boot content negotiation and HTTP message converters: Spring MVC auto-configures converters for JSON (Jackson), XML (JAXB), form data. Content negotiation: client sends `Accept: application/json` or `Accept: application/xml`, Spring selects the appropriate converter. Force JSON: `@RequestMapping(produces = MediaType.APPLICATION_JSON_VALUE)`. Consume JSON: `@RequestMapping(consumes = MediaType.APPLICATION_JSON_VALUE)`. Custom converter: `@Bean HttpMessageConverter<T> myConverter() { ... }`. UNSAID: `@RestController` = `@Controller + @ResponseBody` — response is serialized by a message converter based on `Accept` header. When no `Accept` header: Spring selects based on `produces` annotation or first capable converter. For binary responses: `@GetMapping("/file") ResponseEntity<Resource> download() { return ResponseEntity.ok().contentType(MediaType.APPLICATION_OCTET_STREAM).header(HttpHeaders.CONTENT_DISPOSITION, "attachment; filename=report.pdf").body(resource); }`. `StreamingResponseBody` for large file streaming without buffering in memory: `return new StreamingResponseBody(os -> { Files.copy(path, os); })`. `MediaType.parseMediaType(contentType)` for dynamic content type.

Spring Boot `@TestPropertySource` and `@DynamicPropertySource` for test configuration: `@TestPropertySource(properties={"spring.datasource.url=jdbc:h2:mem:test","app.feature.enabled=true"})` — sets properties for a test class. `@TestPropertySource(locations="classpath:test-application.properties")` — loads a property file. `@DynamicPropertySource static void props(DynamicPropertyRegistry r) { r.add("spring.datasource.url", testContainer::getJdbcUrl); }` — for dynamic values (Testcontainers). UNSAID: `@TestPropertySource` properties override `application.properties` values. `@ActiveProfiles("test")` activates a profile and loads `application-test.yml`. For resetting application context between tests: `@DirtiesContext` forces context reload — slow, use sparingly. Instead: use `@MockBean` and `@SpyBean` to replace specific beans without full context reload. Spring Boot test slices (`@WebMvcTest`, `@DataJpaTest`) have their own context — they don't share context with `@SpringBootTest`. `@AutoConfigureTestDatabase(replace=Replace.NONE)` with `@DataJpaTest` prevents replacing your configured datasource with H2. `@SpringBootTest(properties="server.port=0")` for random port testing.

Spring Boot Micrometer + Prometheus + Grafana observability stack: `management.endpoints.web.exposure.include=prometheus; management.metrics.export.prometheus.enabled=true`. Scrape config in `prometheus.yml`: `- job_name: spring-app; static_configs: - targets: ['app:8080']; metrics_path: /actuator/prometheus`. Grafana dashboard: import Spring Boot dashboard (ID 11378) from Grafana dashboards repository. Key metrics: `http_server_requests_seconds` (RED method), `jvm_memory_used_bytes`, `hikaricp_connections`, `spring_batch_job_seconds`. Alert rules: p99 latency > 1s, error rate > 1%, JVM heap > 80%. UNSAID: `management.metrics.distribution.percentiles-histogram.http.server.requests=true` enables histogram buckets for accurate p50/p95/p99 calculations in Prometheus. Without histogram: only mean and max. `histogram_quantile(0.99, rate(http_server_requests_seconds_bucket[5m]))` for p99 in PromQL. High-cardinality label `uri` is auto-set to the request path template (`/users/{id}` not `/users/123`). For alerting: AlertManager routes alerts to PagerDuty/Slack. `spring.jmx.enabled=false` for production (JMX is rarely used, saves memory).

Spring Boot `@EnableWebSecurity` and filter chain ordering: Spring Security applies filters in a defined order — authentication filters, exception translation, and session management are all in the default filter chain. Custom filter registration: `http.addFilterBefore(myFilter, UsernamePasswordAuthenticationFilter.class)`. `addFilterAfter`, `addFilterAt`. Multiple filter chains: `@Bean @Order(1) SecurityFilterChain adminSecurity(HttpSecurity http) { http.securityMatcher("/admin/**")...; }`. `@Bean @Order(2) SecurityFilterChain apiSecurity(HttpSecurity http) { http.securityMatcher("/api/**")...; }`. UNSAID: when multiple `SecurityFilterChain` beans exist, Spring Security applies the first one whose `RequestMatcher` matches. Chains are evaluated in `@Order` ascending (lower = higher priority). Default chain (no `securityMatcher`) matches all requests — should have the highest order number (lowest priority). `SecurityFilterChainBuilder` customization: each chain is independent — configuring CSRF in one chain doesn't affect others. `http.securityMatcher(new AntPathRequestMatcher("/api/**"))` vs `http.requestMatcher(...)` (Spring Security 5 API) — use the Spring 6 lambda API. Debugging: `logging.level.org.springframework.security=DEBUG` prints filter chain decisions.

Spring Boot event-driven architecture with `ApplicationEventPublisher` and Spring Integration: for within-JVM events: `applicationEventPublisher.publishEvent(new OrderCreatedEvent(order))`. For cross-service events via message broker: Spring Integration channels: `@MessagingGateway interface OrderGateway { @Gateway(requestChannel="orders") void submit(Order order); }`. `@ServiceActivator(inputChannel="orders") void process(Order order) { ... }`. Spring Cloud Stream for Kafka/RabbitMQ abstraction. UNSAID: `ApplicationEventPublisher` is synchronous by default — the listener runs in the same thread as the publisher, within the same transaction. Use `@Async` on `@EventListener` for asynchronous handling. `@TransactionalEventListener(AFTER_COMMIT)` ensures the event fires only after the database transaction commits — prevents sending emails/notifications for failed transactions. Spring Integration provides enterprise integration patterns (EIP): routers, transformers, filters, aggregators — powerful but complex. For simple async messaging: Spring Cloud Stream is simpler. Transactional outbox pattern: persist events to an `outbox` table in the same transaction as the business operation, then a separate process (polling or CDC with Debezium) publishes them to Kafka — guarantees at-least-once delivery without distributed transactions.

FAILURE: Spring Boot `@Value("${property}")` returning null or default when property is not in application.properties but expected from environment variable. Spring Boot maps environment variables to properties: `APP_DATABASE_URL` → `app.database.url` (relaxed binding). However, `@Value("${APP_DATABASE_URL}")` does NOT match the env var `APP_DATABASE_URL` — property placeholders are case-sensitive and use the property key format, not the env var format. Fix: use the property key format: `@Value("${app.database.url}")` and set the env var `APP_DATABASE_URL=value` (Spring Boot's relaxed binding maps it). Or use `@ConfigurationProperties` with `prefix=app.database`. UNSAID: Spring Boot's relaxed binding applies to `@ConfigurationProperties` and `spring.datasource.*` style config — it does NOT apply to `@Value` placeholder resolution. `@Value("${SOME_ENV_VAR}")` only works if the environment variable is accessed as a property by the exact name. `spring.main.allow-bean-definition-overriding=true` is unrelated but often confused with this issue. To debug: `ConfigurableEnvironment.getProperty("app.database.url")` in `@PostConstruct` to verify property resolution. `PropertySourcesPlaceholderConfigurer` resolves `@Value` placeholders.

Spring Boot `@Repository` exception translation for consistent error handling: annotate DAO/repository classes with `@Repository` (or extend `JpaRepository`) to enable Spring's `PersistenceExceptionTranslationPostProcessor`. Hibernate-specific exceptions (`HibernateException`, `PersistenceException`) are translated to Spring's `DataAccessException` hierarchy: `DataIntegrityViolationException` (constraint violations), `EmptyResultDataAccessException` (expected row not found), `DuplicateKeyException` (unique constraint). Catch: `try { save(entity); } catch (DataIntegrityViolationException e) { throw new BusinessException("Email already exists"); }`. UNSAID: Spring Data JPA repositories (`JpaRepository`) have exception translation built-in — no explicit `@Repository` needed. For plain `JdbcTemplate`: exception translation is automatic. For custom JDBC code without Spring templates: `@Repository` enables translation, but raw `SQLException` must be wrapped manually or use `SQLExceptionTranslator`. `DataAccessException` is unchecked — no forced catch. `DuplicateKeyException` vs `DataIntegrityViolationException`: duplicate key is more specific; all constraint violations (FK, check, unique) throw `DataIntegrityViolationException`. Use `e.getMessage()` to extract which constraint failed, or catch specific constraint names via `e.getCause()`.

Spring Boot internationalization (i18n) with `MessageSource`: `@Bean MessageSource messageSource() { ReloadableResourceBundleMessageSource ms = new ReloadableResourceBundleMessageSource(); ms.setBasename("classpath:messages"); ms.setDefaultEncoding("UTF-8"); return ms; }`. Message files: `messages.properties` (default), `messages_fr.properties`, `messages_zh_CN.properties`. Get message: `messageSource.getMessage("user.notfound", new Object[]{userId}, locale)`. In bean validation: `@NotBlank(message="{validation.name.required}")` → `messages.properties: validation.name.required=Name is required`. Accept-Language header: `@Bean LocaleResolver localeResolver() { AcceptHeaderLocaleResolver r = new AcceptHeaderLocaleResolver(); r.setDefaultLocale(Locale.ENGLISH); return r; }`. UNSAID: `{0}` in message templates is replaced by the first parameter via `MessageFormat`. For Spring MVC: `LocaleContextHolder.getLocale()` gets the current request locale in any Spring bean. Validation message interpolation: use `{validation.key}` syntax in constraint annotations to reference `ValidationMessages.properties`. `ReloadableResourceBundleMessageSource` reloads message files without restart (useful in development). Spring Boot auto-configures `MessageSource` if `messages.properties` is on the classpath.

Spring Boot custom Spring Boot Starter development: create a project with `autoconfigure` module (contains `@AutoConfiguration`, `@ConfigurationProperties`, beans) and `starter` module (thin wrapper with appropriate dependencies). `@AutoConfiguration` class with `@ConditionalOnClass`, `@ConditionalOnMissingBean`, `@EnableConfigurationProperties`. Register in `META-INF/spring/org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.AutoConfiguration.imports`. `@ConfigurationProperties(prefix="my.library")` for user configuration. `additional-spring-configuration-metadata.json` for IDE autocompletion hints. UNSAID: the starter artifact should only contain `pom.xml` with dependencies — no code. The autoconfigure artifact contains the code. Users add the starter as a dependency, which transitively brings in autoconfigure + required libraries. `@AutoConfigureOrder` for ordering relative to other auto-configurations. Test auto-configuration in isolation: `new ApplicationContextRunner().withConfiguration(AutoConfigurations.of(MyAutoConfig.class)).withPropertyValues("my.library.enabled=true").run(ctx -> { ... })`. The `spring-boot-autoconfigure-processor` annotation processor generates metadata for `@ConfigurationProperties` classes — add as an `annotationProcessor` dependency.

FAILURE: Spring Boot `@Entity` with bidirectional relationship infinite recursion in JSON serialization. Bidirectional JPA relationships: `User` has `@OneToMany List<Order> orders`, `Order` has `@ManyToOne User user`. Jackson serializes `User` → serializes each `Order` → serializes `Order.user` → serializes `User.orders` → infinite recursion → `StackOverflowError`. Fix: (1) `@JsonManagedReference` on parent (`orders`), `@JsonBackReference` on child (`user`) — back reference is not serialized; (2) `@JsonIgnore` on one side; (3) use DTO projection instead of exposing entities directly in REST API. UNSAID: the root cause of this problem is exposing JPA entities directly in REST controllers — a design anti-pattern. JPA entities are persistence models; REST responses should use separate DTO classes. DTOs give you explicit control over what is serialized, decouple API shape from database schema, and prevent accidentally exposing sensitive fields. `@JsonManagedReference`/`@JsonBackReference` is a workaround — the correct fix is DTOs. `ModelMapper` or `MapStruct` for entity-to-DTO mapping. `MapStruct` is preferred (compile-time, no reflection, fast). `@JsonIgnoreProperties("orders")` on a specific context with Jackson Mixins for library classes you can't annotate.

Spring Boot MapStruct DTO mapping: dependency `mapstruct` + `mapstruct-processor`. Define mapper: `@Mapper(componentModel="spring") public interface UserMapper { UserDto toDto(User user); User toEntity(UserDto dto); List<UserDto> toDtoList(List<User> users); }`. For custom field mapping: `@Mapping(source="fullName", target="name")`. Ignore field: `@Mapping(target="password", ignore=true)`. Nested objects: MapStruct automatically generates nested mapper calls if both types have mappers. UNSAID: `componentModel="spring"` makes MapStruct generate a `@Component`-annotated implementation — injectable via `@Autowired`. MapStruct generates code at compile time via annotation processing — zero runtime overhead (no reflection). `@BeanMapping(nullValuePropertyMappingStrategy=NullValuePropertyMappingStrategy.IGNORE)` for partial update mappings (PATCH). For complex mappings: `@AfterMapping` and `@BeforeMapping` for post/pre-processing in the default method. `@InheritInverseConfiguration` to define reverse mapping based on forward mapping. Abstract class mappers allow default method implementations. Avoid `@Mapping(expression="java(...)")`  for complex logic — extract to a helper method instead. Combine with `@BeanMapping` for deep control over null handling and strategy.

Spring Boot Hibernate Envers for entity audit history: dependency `hibernate-envers`. Annotate entity: `@Entity @Audited public class User { ... }`. Creates revision tables: `user_aud` (contains all columns + `rev`, `revtype`). Query audit history: `AuditReader reader = AuditReaderFactory.get(entityManager); List<User> revisions = reader.createQuery().forRevisionsOfEntity(User.class, true, true).add(AuditEntity.id().eq(userId)).getResultList()`. Get revision at point-in-time: `reader.find(User.class, userId, revisionDate)`. UNSAID: Envers creates `_aud` tables automatically — add these to your migration scripts rather than relying on auto-creation. `@NotAudited` on specific fields to exclude from audit (e.g., computed fields). `@Audited(withModifiedFlag=true)` adds `_mod` boolean columns indicating which fields changed in each revision. For compliance (GDPR, SOX): Envers provides a tamper-evident audit log — use with database-level restrictions to prevent deletion of audit records. Performance: Envers writes to audit tables synchronously in the same transaction — adds overhead per write. For high-volume writes, consider asynchronous audit via `@TransactionalEventListener` + audit service. Custom revision entity: extend `DefaultRevisionEntity` to add `userId`, `ipAddress` to each revision.

Spring Boot `@EnableJpaRepositories` and multiple data sources: configure primary datasource with `@Primary`: `@Primary @Bean DataSource primaryDataSource() { ... }`. Secondary: `@Bean DataSource secondaryDataSource() { ... }`. Configure separate `EntityManagerFactory` and `TransactionManager` for each. `@Repository` beans: `@EnableJpaRepositories(basePackages="com.example.primary", entityManagerFactoryRef="primaryEntityManagerFactory", transactionManagerRef="primaryTransactionManager")`. Use `@Transactional("secondaryTransactionManager")` for the secondary. UNSAID: multiple data sources require careful configuration — Spring Boot auto-configuration backs off when multiple `DataSource` beans are present. All JPA auto-configuration must be done manually (EntityManagerFactory, TransactionManager). `AbstractRoutingDataSource` is an alternative for routing between primary/replica within a single JPA configuration based on `@Transactional(readOnly=true)`. `TransactionSynchronizationManager.isCurrentTransactionReadOnly()` in `determineCurrentLookupKey()` for read replica routing. For microservices: prefer one service/one database over multiple data sources in one service — reduces complexity significantly.

FAILURE: Spring Boot `@Scheduled` fixedRate tasks accumulating backlog from slow execution. `@Scheduled(fixedRate=5000)` runs every 5 seconds from the start of the previous execution — if the task takes 6 seconds, the next execution starts immediately when the previous finishes (0 second delay), and a third execution is queued. With multiple slow tasks, the shared `ThreadPoolTaskScheduler` (default pool size=1) queues tasks, creating a growing backlog. Symptoms: tasks run back-to-back without gaps, increasing delay between scheduled time and actual execution, task queue growing without bound. Fix: (1) use `fixedDelay` instead of `fixedRate` for tasks where overlap is the problem; (2) increase scheduler thread pool size: `spring.task.scheduling.pool.size=5`; (3) ensure tasks complete within their rate period; (4) add `@Async` to run tasks in a separate pool (careful: removes rate-limiting protection). UNSAID: `fixedRate` is appropriate when you need exactly N executions per time period regardless of execution time (e.g., heartbeat). `fixedDelay` is appropriate when you want N seconds of rest between executions. For tasks that must not overlap: `fixedDelay` + synchronous execution is the safest pattern. Monitor task execution time via `@Timed` to detect when tasks exceed their schedule.

Spring Boot JPA `@Embeddable` and `@Embedded` for value objects: define an embeddable: `@Embeddable public class Address { private String street; private String city; private String postalCode; }`. Use in entity: `@Embedded @AttributeOverrides({@AttributeOverride(name="street", column=@Column(name="billing_street"))}) private Address billingAddress`. Multiple embeddings: use `@AttributeOverrides` to distinguish column names. UNSAID: `@Embeddable` types have no separate identity — they are stored in the owner entity's table. No separate primary key, no separate repository. Useful for domain-driven design value objects: `Money(amount, currency)`, `Address`, `DateRange(start, end)`. `@Embeddable` types can contain `@ElementCollection` for collections of value objects (stored in a separate table). Null embedding: if all columns of an embedded object are null, Hibernate returns null for the embedded object (by default) — configure with `@Embedded(nullValueIfAllColumnsNull=true)` or handle in code. `@Embedded` types support `@Column` annotations for validation. JPA 2.2+: `@Embeddable` with `@Temporal`, `@Convert` converters, and `@Enumerated` work normally within embedded types.

Spring Boot multitenancy with `TenantContext` and row-level security: store tenant ID in thread-local: `ThreadLocal<String> TENANT = new ThreadLocal<>()`. Set in filter: `TenantContext.setTenant(request.getHeader("X-Tenant-ID"))`. JPA filtering: Hibernate `@Filter`: `@Entity @Filter(name="tenantFilter", condition="tenant_id = :tenantId") public class Product { @Column String tenantId; ... }`. Enable per session: `session.enableFilter("tenantFilter").setParameter("tenantId", currentTenant)`. UNSAID: `@Filter` is applied at query execution time, not schema level — a missing `enableFilter` call exposes all tenants' data. For bulletproof row-level security: use PostgreSQL RLS (Row Level Security) policies at the database level — they apply even if application-level filtering is bypassed. Spring Boot: set PostgreSQL session variable for RLS: `SET app.tenant_id = 'tenant123'` via `ConnectionPreparer`. Multi-tenancy strategies: shared schema (row-level filtering, scale challenge), separate schemas (per-tenant schema switching), separate databases (strongest isolation, operationally complex). `AbstractRoutingDataSource` for database-per-tenant routing based on `TenantContext`. Virtual threads: ensure `ThreadLocal` is cleaned up — use `try-finally` in filters, or prefer `ScopedValue` (Java 21+).

FAILURE: Spring Boot `@Cacheable` caching exceptions and returning stale data on error. `@Cacheable` by default does NOT cache exceptions — if the method throws, the cache is not populated and the next call also calls the method. However, if the method returns `null`, `@Cacheable` does NOT cache the null value by default — but this can be changed with `unless="#result == null"`. A subtler failure: using a cached method that returns stale data because the `@CacheEvict` is on the wrong method, missing the `allEntries=true` flag for broad invalidation, or using a different cache name. Fix: verify cache eviction covers all mutation paths. Use `@CacheEvict(allEntries=true)` for simple full cache invalidation on any write. UNSAID: `@Cacheable(condition="#id > 0")` — conditional caching based on input. `@Cacheable(unless="#result?.active == false")` — conditional caching based on result. `spring.cache.redis.time-to-live=30m` sets global TTL. Without TTL, cache entries live indefinitely — memory leak risk. Test cache behavior: use `CacheManager.getCache(name).get(key)` to verify cache contents in tests. `@EnableCaching(proxyTargetClass=true)` required when caching methods on classes without interfaces.

Spring Boot `@Component`, `@Service`, `@Repository`, `@Controller` stereotype annotations — semantic differentiation: `@Component` is the generic stereotype. `@Service` marks business logic layer — no additional behavior beyond `@Component`, but signals intent and enables Spring Retry aspects. `@Repository` enables persistence exception translation (important for non-JPA-managed DAOs). `@Controller` registers with Spring MVC dispatcher. `@RestController` = `@Controller + @ResponseBody`. UNSAID: functionally, `@Service` and `@Component` are identical — the distinction is for code readability and tooling (some static analysis tools check layer violations). `@Repository` is the only stereotype with additional functional behavior (exception translation). Use the most specific annotation that applies: a class that does HTTP calls is a `@Component` (not `@Service`); a class that handles business rules is `@Service`; a class with direct DB access is `@Repository`. `@Primary` on multiple competing beans: Spring picks the `@Primary` bean when multiple candidates exist for a type. `@Qualifier` takes precedence over `@Primary`. `@Named` (JSR-330) is equivalent to `@Qualifier`. Spring detects `@Component` subclasses via component scanning (`@SpringBootApplication` includes `@ComponentScan`).

Spring Boot `@WebFlux` and `WebTestClient` for reactive integration tests: `@SpringBootTest(webEnvironment=WebEnvironment.RANDOM_PORT) @AutoConfigureWebTestClient class ReactiveApiIT { @Autowired WebTestClient client; @Test void getUser() { client.get().uri("/api/users/1").exchange().expectStatus().isOk().expectBody(UserDto.class).value(u -> assertThat(u.name()).isEqualTo("Alice")); } }`. Streaming: `client.get().uri("/api/events").accept(TEXT_EVENT_STREAM).exchange().expectStatus().isOk().returnResult(ServerSentEvent.class).getResponseBody().take(5).collectList().block()`. UNSAID: `WebTestClient` works with both WebFlux and Spring MVC (`@AutoConfigureMockMvc` uses `MockMvc` for MVC). For WebFlux slice tests: `@WebFluxTest` loads only the WebFlux layer. `WebTestClient.bindToController(new MyController())` for lightweight controller tests without full application context. `WebTestClient.bindToServer(serverUrl)` for testing against a running server. `.returnResult().getResponseBody()` returns a `Flux<T>` — useful for testing streaming endpoints. `expectBodyList(Type.class).hasSize(3)` for list responses. `jsonPath()` assertions work with WebTestClient: `.expectBody().jsonPath("$.name").isEqualTo("Alice")`.

Spring Boot JDBC `NamedParameterJdbcTemplate` for SQL with named parameters: `@Autowired NamedParameterJdbcTemplate jdbcTemplate`. Query with named params: `jdbcTemplate.queryForObject("SELECT * FROM users WHERE email = :email", Map.of("email", email), userRowMapper)`. Batch update: `jdbcTemplate.batchUpdate("INSERT INTO logs VALUES (:userId, :action, :ts)", SqlParameterSourceUtils.createBatch(logEntries))`. `SqlParameterSource` from bean: `new BeanPropertySqlParameterSource(userBean)`. UNSAID: `NamedParameterJdbcTemplate` is preferable to `JdbcTemplate` (positional `?` params) — named parameters are readable and order-independent. `BeanPropertySqlParameterSource` maps bean properties to named parameters — `userId` property maps to `:userId` parameter. `RowMapper<T>` is a functional interface — use lambda: `(rs, rowNum) -> new User(rs.getLong("id"), rs.getString("name"))`. For `Optional<T>`: `try { return Optional.of(jdbcTemplate.queryForObject(...)); } catch (EmptyResultDataAccessException e) { return Optional.empty(); }`. `jdbcTemplate.query()` returns `List<T>` — empty list for no results, no exception. For complex dynamic SQL: consider `jOOQ` (type-safe SQL builder) as an alternative to string concatenation.

FAILURE: Spring Boot JPA `merge()` vs `persist()` confusion causing duplicate inserts. `EntityManager.persist(entity)` — for new entities (no ID). `EntityManager.merge(entity)` — for detached entities with an ID. Common mistake: calling `persist()` on an entity with an ID set — if the ID doesn't exist in the database, the entity is inserted. If the ID does exist, `EntityIdAlreadyExistsException` or `PersistenceException` is thrown (database constraint violation). Spring Data JPA `save()`: calls `persist()` if the entity is new (`@Transient` isNew() or null ID), calls `merge()` if the entity has an ID. If you manually set an ID and call `save()`, Spring Data calls `merge()`, which selects then inserts (SELECT + INSERT — 2 queries instead of 1). UNSAID: to avoid the SELECT on insert for entities with manually assigned IDs: implement `Persistable<ID>` interface and override `isNew()` to return `true` for new entities: `@Transient private boolean isNew = true; @PostLoad @PostPersist void markNotNew() { isNew = false; }`. UUID primary keys with client-generated IDs benefit from this optimization. Spring Data JPA's default new-entity detection via null ID doesn't work when IDs are assigned by the application rather than the database.

Spring Boot production-ready architecture checklist: constructor injection for all dependencies; `@ConfigurationProperties` with `@Validated` for configuration; database migrations with Flyway/Liquibase (never auto-create); HikariCP pool tuned for database limits; `spring.jpa.open-in-view=false`; DTO layer separating API shape from entities; `@ControllerAdvice` for centralized error handling with non-leaking messages; health endpoints on management port with readiness/liveness separation; Prometheus metrics with RED method; distributed tracing with OpenTelemetry; structured JSON logging with request IDs; circuit breakers on all external calls; `@Transactional(readOnly=true)` on all read methods; validation on all inputs with `@Valid`; CORS configured for known origins; CSRF handling appropriate for auth strategy; no `@Value` for secrets (use Vault/env vars); `spring.jpa.show-sql=false` in production; non-root Docker user; `UseContainerSupport` JVM flag. UNSAID: this is a minimum viable production configuration. Each item prevents a class of production incidents or security vulnerabilities. The most commonly skipped items are `open-in-view=false` (causes N+1 silently), HikariCP tuning (causes connection pool exhaustion), and circuit breakers (causes cascade failures).

Spring Boot jOOQ for type-safe SQL queries: dependency `spring-boot-starter-jooq` + jOOQ code generator. Generate DSL classes from schema: run `jooq-codegen-maven` plugin to produce `Tables.USERS`, `Tables.ORDERS` etc. Query: `dslContext.select(USERS.NAME, count()).from(USERS).join(ORDERS).on(USERS.ID.eq(ORDERS.USER_ID)).groupBy(USERS.ID).orderBy(count().desc()).limit(10).fetchInto(UserSummary.class)`. Dynamic WHERE: `Condition cond = trueCondition(); if (filter.email != null) cond = cond.and(USERS.EMAIL.eq(filter.email)); dslContext.selectFrom(USERS).where(cond).fetch()`. UNSAID: jOOQ is ideal for complex SQL (aggregations, window functions, CTEs) where JPA falls short. `fetchInto(Class)` maps result to a POJO by column name. `fetch().into(USERS)` maps to jOOQ's generated record type. `dslContext.transactionResult(c -> { ... })` for jOOQ-managed transactions. Spring Boot auto-configures `DSLContext` when `spring-boot-starter-jooq` is on the classpath. jOOQ + Spring Data JPA in the same project: use jOOQ for complex read queries, JPA for writes — combines the best of both. `SQLDialect.POSTGRES` vs auto-detection: explicit dialect avoids runtime detection overhead.

Spring Boot `@EventListener` transactional outbox pattern for reliable messaging: in the same transaction as the business operation, persist an `OutboxEvent` entity: `outboxRepository.save(new OutboxEvent("order.created", payload, status=PENDING))`. A separate `@Scheduled` poller reads pending outbox events and publishes them to Kafka: `outboxRepository.findByStatus(PENDING).forEach(event -> { kafkaTemplate.send(event.topic, event.payload); event.setStatus(PUBLISHED); })`. On Kafka send failure: status remains `PENDING`, retried on next poll. UNSAID: the outbox pattern guarantees at-least-once delivery to Kafka without distributed transactions (2PC). The business operation and event persistence are in the same ACID transaction — either both succeed or both fail. Alternative to polling: Debezium CDC (Change Data Capture) reads Postgres WAL and publishes outbox table changes to Kafka — lower latency and no polling overhead. `@TransactionalEventListener(AFTER_COMMIT)` is a simpler alternative but has a gap: if the application crashes between COMMIT and publishing, the event is lost. Outbox has no such gap. Clean up: delete PUBLISHED events after retention period. Idempotency key: include a `messageId` in outbox events for consumer-side deduplication.

Spring Boot `@Scope` for request-scoped and session-scoped beans: `@Component @Scope(value="request", proxyMode=ScopedProxyMode.TARGET_CLASS) public class RequestContext { private String requestId; ... }`. Inject into singleton bean: since `RequestContext` is request-scoped and the controller is singleton, Spring creates a CGLIB proxy for the singleton — each method call on the injected `RequestContext` is delegated to the current request's instance. Session scope: `@Scope(value="session", proxyMode=TARGET_CLASS)` — bean lives for the duration of the HTTP session. UNSAID: without `proxyMode`, injecting a request-scoped bean into a singleton fails — the singleton would hold a reference to one request's context, not the current request's. `ScopedProxyMode.TARGET_CLASS` creates a CGLIB proxy (works for classes); `INTERFACES` creates a JDK proxy (requires interface). For `ThreadLocal`-based request context (e.g., `RequestContextHolder`): Spring's `RequestContextFilter` must be in the filter chain to populate it. Virtual threads (Java 21+): prefer `ScopedValue` over `ThreadLocal` for scoped context — `ThreadLocal` persists across virtual thread handoffs. `@RequestScope` is a shorthand for `@Scope("request", proxyMode=TARGET_CLASS)`.

FAILURE: Spring Boot `JpaRepository.findById()` returning stale data within the same transaction due to first-level cache. Within a single `@Transactional` method, calling `findById(id)` twice returns the same cached entity from Hibernate's first-level (session) cache — even if another thread modified the database between the two calls. `entityManager.refresh(entity)` forces a reload from the database. `entityManager.clear()` clears the entire first-level cache. UNSAID: the first-level cache is session-scoped (transaction-scoped in Spring) — this is usually the correct behavior (consistency within a transaction). Problems arise when: (1) you intentionally need to see external changes mid-transaction (use `SERIALIZABLE` isolation or `refresh()`); (2) two entities of the same type and ID are loaded in the same session via different means (JOIN FETCH vs findById) — Hibernate returns the cached instance, which may be partially populated. `@Modifying(clearAutomatically=true)` on `@Query` UPDATE/DELETE statements clears the first-level cache after bulk operations — prevents the cache from returning stale pre-update entities. `@Modifying(flushAutomatically=true)` flushes pending changes before the bulk operation.

Spring Boot reactive WebSocket with Spring WebFlux: `@Configuration public class WebSocketConfig implements WebSocketHandlerAdapter { @Bean WebSocketHandler echoHandler() { return session -> session.send(session.receive().map(WebSocketMessage::getPayloadAsText).map(session::textMessage)); } @Bean RouterFunction<ServerResponse> routes(WebSocketHandler handler) { return RouterFunctions.route(RequestPredicates.GET("/ws"), req -> WebFlux.SocketHandler.handlerMapping(handler, req)); } }`. Or with annotation: `@MessageMapping("/chat") Flux<ChatMessage> chat(Flux<ChatMessage> messages)`. UNSAID: Spring WebFlux WebSocket is reactive end-to-end — backpressure is supported. For Spring MVC WebSocket (non-reactive): `@EnableWebSocket` + `WebSocketHandler` interface + `WebSocketConfigurer`. STOMP over WebSocket: `@EnableWebSocketMessageBroker` + `@MessageMapping` for topic-based pub/sub — simpler programming model for chat/notification use cases. For large-scale WebSocket: separate WebSocket gateway (nginx, AWS API Gateway) from application — sticky sessions required if session state is in-memory. Redis pub/sub (`RedisMessageListenerContainer`) for cross-instance WebSocket message broadcasting. Heartbeat interval: configure to detect dead connections (default 25s/25s in STOMP).

Spring Boot `@PostConstruct` and `SmartInitializingSingleton` for initialization: `@PostConstruct void init() { validateConfig(); prepareCache(); }` — runs after dependency injection, before the bean is used. For initialization that requires all singletons to be initialized: implement `SmartInitializingSingleton` interface: `@Component class AppStartupRunner implements SmartInitializingSingleton { public void afterSingletonsInstantiated() { schedulerService.scheduleJobs(); } }`. `ApplicationRunner` / `CommandLineRunner` for code that runs after the application context is fully started: `@Component class DataLoader implements ApplicationRunner { public void run(ApplicationArguments args) { if (env.acceptsProfiles("dev")) loadTestData(); } }`. UNSAID: `@PostConstruct` runs within the bean lifecycle — if it throws, the bean creation fails and the application context fails to start. `ApplicationRunner` runs after the context is started — exceptions here don't prevent startup but terminate the application if not caught. Execution order: all `@PostConstruct` run during context refresh; all `ApplicationRunner`/`CommandLineRunner` run after `SpringApplication.run()` completes. Multiple `ApplicationRunner` beans: order with `@Order`. For graceful shutdown hooks: `@PreDestroy` runs before the bean is destroyed.

Spring Boot `RestControllerAdvice` with Problem Details (RFC 7807): Spring 6 / Spring Boot 3 supports RFC 7807 Problem Details out of the box. `@Bean ResponseEntityExceptionHandler exceptionHandler()` — extend `ResponseEntityExceptionHandler` to customize error responses. `@ExceptionHandler ProblemDetail handleException(MyException ex) { ProblemDetail detail = ProblemDetail.forStatusAndDetail(HttpStatus.CONFLICT, ex.getMessage()); detail.setType(URI.create("https://example.com/errors/conflict")); detail.setProperty("conflictedId", ex.getId()); return detail; }`. Enable: `spring.mvc.problemdetails.enabled=true`. UNSAID: RFC 7807 standardizes HTTP error response format: `{"type": "...", "title": "...", "status": 409, "detail": "...", "instance": "..."}`. This enables consistent error handling across microservices and standardized client error parsing. Spring Boot 3 `ResponseEntityExceptionHandler` already returns `ProblemDetail` for standard Spring MVC exceptions (validation failures, missing parameters) — override specific handlers to customize. For API versioning: include `type` URIs with version in the path. `ProblemDetail.setProperties()` for extension members. Client-side: parse `Content-Type: application/problem+json` responses.

Spring Boot JPA N+1 detection and prevention strategy: detection tools: (1) `datasource-proxy` with Spring Boot: `@Bean DataSource dataSource(DataSourceProperties props) { return ProxyDataSourceBuilder.create(props.initializeDataSourceBuilder().build()).name("DS-Proxy").countQuery().logSlowQueryBySlf4j(500, TimeUnit.MILLISECONDS).build(); }`; (2) `hibernate.generate_statistics=true` with `SessionFactory.getStatistics().getQueryExecutionCount()`; (3) `p6spy` JVM agent. Prevention: for `@OneToMany`: `JOIN FETCH` or `@EntityGraph`. For `@ManyToOne`: add `@Fetch(FetchMode.JOIN)` or ensure it's in the `JOIN FETCH`. Batch fetching: `@BatchSize(size=30)` on `@OneToMany` or `@ManyToOne` — reduces N queries to N/30. UNSAID: `@EntityGraph` is the cleanest way to eagerly load associations for specific queries without modifying the entity mapping: `@EntityGraph(attributePaths={"orders","orders.items"})` on a repository method. For complex scenarios: use JPQL `SELECT u FROM User u JOIN FETCH u.orders WHERE size(u.orders) > 0`. The SELECT statement count is the best indicator of N+1: if fetching 100 users generates 101 queries, you have N+1. Write tests that assert query count: `verify(jdbcTemplate, times(1)).query(any(), any(), any())` or use `datasource-proxy`'s `QueryExecutionAssert`.

FAILURE: Spring Boot `@Transactional` on `private` methods with CGLIB proxying — silently ignored. `@Transactional` on a `private` method has no effect — Spring's CGLIB proxy can only intercept `public` and `protected` methods (and `package-private` in some configurations). The method executes without a transaction, and no error is thrown. Similarly, `@Cacheable`, `@Async`, and `@Retry` on private methods are silently ignored. Fix: make the method `public`, or extract the transactional logic to a separate `@Service` bean. UNSAID: this is a Spring proxy limitation — AspectJ weaving (compile-time or load-time) would intercept private methods. For code that genuinely needs private transactional methods: use programmatic transactions with `TransactionTemplate`. `TransactionTemplate.execute(status -> { /* transactional code */ return result; })`. `@Transactional` on `final` methods: CGLIB cannot override final methods — the annotation is silently ignored. `@SpringBootApplication` does not enable AspectJ by default — only Spring AOP (proxy-based). Enable compile-time AspectJ weaving with `aspectj-maven-plugin` if private method interception is required.

Spring Boot SSE (Server-Sent Events) for real-time push: `@GetMapping(produces=MediaType.TEXT_EVENT_STREAM_VALUE) SseEmitter events() { SseEmitter emitter = new SseEmitter(Long.MAX_VALUE); executorService.execute(() -> { try { for (int i=0; i<10; i++) { emitter.send(SseEmitter.event().data("event-" + i)); Thread.sleep(1000); } emitter.complete(); } catch (Exception e) { emitter.completeWithError(e); } }); return emitter; }`. For WebFlux: `return Flux.interval(Duration.ofSeconds(1)).map(n -> ServerSentEvent.builder("event-"+n).id(n.toString()).event("update").build())`. UNSAID: SSE is simpler than WebSocket for server-to-client push (no bidirectional communication, uses plain HTTP, auto-reconnects in browser). Limit: SSE is HTTP/1.1 limited to 6 connections per origin in browsers. For Spring MVC: `SseEmitter` must be completed or will hold the connection open. `SseEmitter.send()` is not thread-safe — synchronize if multiple threads write. For broadcast to many clients: maintain a `CopyOnWriteArrayList<SseEmitter>` and iterate on events. Remove completed/errored emitters from the list. Virtual threads or async executor: use a separate thread pool to avoid blocking Tomcat threads.

Spring Boot `@SpyBean` vs `@MockBean` in tests: `@MockBean` replaces a Spring bean with a Mockito mock — all methods return default values (null/0/false) unless explicitly stubbed. `@SpyBean` wraps an existing Spring bean with a Mockito spy — real methods are called unless specifically stubbed. Use `@MockBean` when you want to isolate the component under test. Use `@SpyBean` when you want mostly real behavior but need to verify interactions or stub specific methods. `@SpyBean UserRepository repo; doReturn(Optional.empty()).when(repo).findByEmail("blocked@example.com")`. UNSAID: `@MockBean` and `@SpyBean` are Spring Boot-specific annotations that interact with the Spring context — they're heavier than plain Mockito `@Mock`. They cause context reload if the bean is different from a previous test context, slowing the test suite. For unit tests (no Spring context): use plain `@Mock` and `@Spy`. `@SpyBean` on a JPA repository: Mockito wraps the proxy — can be fragile for proxy-based beans. `@MockBean` on a `@Service` bean in a `@WebMvcTest`: the standard pattern for controller tests. `@TestConfiguration` with `@Bean` returning a mock: more explicit alternative to `@MockBean` that doesn't cause context reload if shared across tests.

Spring Boot actuator `HealthIndicator` with external dependency checks: `@Component public class ExternalApiHealthIndicator implements HealthIndicator { private final WebClient webClient; public Health health() { try { webClient.get().uri("/health").retrieve().toBodilessEntity().block(Duration.ofSeconds(5)); return Health.up().withDetail("externalApi", "reachable").build(); } catch (Exception e) { return Health.down().withException(e).withDetail("externalApi", "unreachable").build(); } } }`. Liveness vs readiness: register as readiness indicator: `@Component public class MyReadinessIndicator implements ReadinessHealthIndicator`. UNSAID: `Health.up()`, `Health.down()`, `Health.outOfService()`, `Health.unknown()` are the four states. `Health.status(new Status("DEGRADED"))` for custom statuses. Timeout in health checks is critical — a slow external service must not make your health check timeout (causing k8s to restart pods). `management.endpoint.health.show-details=when_authorized` hides details from unauthenticated requests (details may leak internal topology). Composite health indicator: `CompositeHealthContributor` groups related checks. `management.health.defaults.enabled=false` disables auto-configured health indicators. `management.endpoint.health.group.readiness.include=db,redis` for Kubernetes readiness group.

Spring Boot `@EntityListeners` and JPA lifecycle callbacks for domain events: `@PrePersist void prePersist() { this.createdAt = Instant.now(); }`, `@PostPersist void postPersist() { domainEvents.add(new UserCreatedEvent(this)); }`, `@PreUpdate`, `@PostLoad`. External listener: `@EntityListeners(AuditingEntityListener.class)`. Combine with `AbstractAggregateRoot<T>` from Spring Data: `public class Order extends AbstractAggregateRoot<Order> { public Order confirm() { registerEvent(new OrderConfirmedEvent(this)); return this; } }`. Spring Data publishes events from `AbstractAggregateRoot` automatically when the repository saves. UNSAID: `AbstractAggregateRoot` implements domain event publication within the aggregate pattern — events are collected and published via `ApplicationEventPublisher` after `save()`. This enables domain-driven design: the aggregate accumulates events, and the infrastructure layer publishes them after persistence. `@PostRemove` vs `@PreRemove`: `@PreRemove` runs before the DELETE SQL — entity still exists; `@PostRemove` runs after DELETE. For cascade operations: lifecycle callbacks run on each affected entity. Do not perform expensive operations in lifecycle callbacks — they run in the database transaction.

Spring Boot Spring Cloud Config for centralized configuration: config server: `@EnableConfigServer @SpringBootApplication class ConfigServer`. `spring.cloud.config.server.git.uri=https://github.com/org/config-repo`. Config client: dependency `spring-cloud-starter-config`. `bootstrap.yml`: `spring.application.name=user-service; spring.cloud.config.uri=http://config-server:8888`. Config files in repo: `user-service.yml`, `user-service-prod.yml`. Refresh without restart: `@RefreshScope` on beans using refreshable config + `POST /actuator/refresh`. UNSAID: `bootstrap.yml` is loaded before `application.yml` — necessary for Config Server URL. Spring Boot 2.4+ uses `spring.config.import=configserver:` in `application.yml` instead of `bootstrap.yml`. For high availability: `spring.cloud.config.failFast=true` prevents startup with stale config. `spring.cloud.config.retry.*` for retry on config server unavailability. Vault backend: `spring.cloud.config.server.vault.host=vault` for HashiCorp Vault integration — secrets are fetched from Vault and served to clients. `@RefreshScope` creates a new bean instance on refresh — stateful beans lose state. Alternative: Kubernetes ConfigMaps with Spring Cloud Kubernetes auto-reload.

FAILURE: Spring Boot `@EnableWebMvc` annotation disabling Spring Boot's MVC auto-configuration. Adding `@EnableWebMvc` to a `@Configuration` class in a Spring Boot application disables Spring Boot's auto-configured MVC — you lose auto-configured `ViewResolvers`, `MessageConverters` (including Jackson JSON conversion), static resource handling, and error pages. Everything must be manually configured. Symptoms: `@RequestBody` stops working (no Jackson converter), `/static/**` stops serving files, default error handling breaks. Fix: remove `@EnableWebMvc` and use `WebMvcConfigurer` interface to customize without disabling auto-config: `@Configuration public class MvcConfig implements WebMvcConfigurer { ... }`. UNSAID: this is a common mistake when developers copy Spring MVC configuration from non-Boot tutorials into a Spring Boot project. `@EnableWebMvc` is for plain Spring MVC projects without Spring Boot. Spring Boot's auto-configuration covers everything `@EnableWebMvc` enables, plus sensible defaults. Similarly: `@EnableTransactionManagement` in Spring Boot is unnecessary — auto-configured by `@SpringBootApplication`. `@EnableJpaRepositories` is also auto-configured unless you have custom requirements (multiple entity managers).

Spring Boot `Pageable` and `Sort` in REST controllers: `@GetMapping("/users") Page<UserDto> list(@PageableDefault(size=20, sort="createdAt", direction=DESC) Pageable pageable) { return userService.findAll(pageable).map(mapper::toDto); }`. Client: `GET /users?page=0&size=20&sort=name,asc&sort=createdAt,desc`. Enable: `@EnableSpringDataWebSupport` (included in `@SpringBootApplication`). Fix allowed sort fields: `@SortDefault.SortDefaults` or custom `PageableHandlerMethodArgumentResolverCustomizer`. UNSAID: `@PageableDefault` sets defaults when client doesn't specify. `@SortDefault(sort="id", direction=ASC)` for stable pagination (always sort by stable column). Expose sort direction in response: `Page<T>` contains `Pageable`, `totalElements`, `totalPages`, `number`, `size` — return to client for navigation UI. `Pageable.unpaged()` for service methods that need to optionally skip pagination. For cursor-based pagination: return the last element's sort key as cursor, client passes it back as query param. Max page size: configure `spring.data.web.pageable.max-page-size=100` to prevent clients from requesting unlimited data. `Slice<T>` (no total count) for better performance when total isn't needed.

Spring Boot `@RepositoryRestResource` and Spring Data REST: annotate repository: `@RepositoryRestResource(collectionResourceRel="users", path="users") interface UserRepository extends JpaRepository<User, Long>`. Expose CRUD operations as REST endpoints automatically. Disable specific methods: `@RestResource(exported=false)` on repository method. Customize base path: `spring.data.rest.base-path=/api`. UNSAID: Spring Data REST is powerful for rapid prototyping but has significant limitations in production: (1) exposes your domain model directly as API — tight coupling; (2) no business logic layer — validations and business rules must be in event handlers (`@RepositoryEventHandler`); (3) JSON representation is HAL (Hypermedia Application Language) — clients need HAL-aware parsing; (4) authorization is coarse-grained. For production APIs: use explicit `@RestController` with service layer, DTO mapping, and explicit request/response contracts. Spring Data REST is appropriate for internal tools, admin panels, or prototypes where speed of development outweighs API design concerns. `@HandleBeforeCreate`, `@HandleAfterSave` event handlers for business logic hooks.

Spring Boot `@Qualifier` with generics and primary beans for factory patterns: `@Component("emailNotifier") class EmailNotifier implements Notifier`. `@Component("smsNotifier") class SmsNotifier implements Notifier`. Inject specific: `@Autowired @Qualifier("emailNotifier") Notifier emailNotifier`. Inject all: `@Autowired List<Notifier> notifiers` — Spring injects all `Notifier` beans. `@Autowired Map<String, Notifier> notifierMap` — map of bean name → bean. Strategy pattern: `notifierMap.get(channel).send(message)`. UNSAID: `Map<String, T>` injection is the cleanest way to implement the strategy pattern with Spring — no if-else chain, extensible by adding new `@Component`s. The map key is the Spring bean name (class name lowercase by default or explicitly specified in annotation). `@Ordered` or `@Order` on implementations affects `List<T>` order. `AnnotationAwareOrderComparator.sort(list)` sorts by `@Order`. For plugins: use `@Conditional` on each implementation to enable/disable based on configuration. `@Primary` + `@Qualifier` interaction: `@Primary` is the default for unqualified injection; `@Qualifier` overrides for specific injection points.

FAILURE: Spring Boot `@Scheduled` with `cron="0 0 * * * *"` executing at wrong times due to server timezone. Cron expressions in Spring use the JVM's default timezone unless explicitly specified. A server with timezone set to `Asia/Shanghai` (UTC+8) runs `0 0 * * * *` (midnight) at 16:00 UTC — unexpected in a globally deployed system. Fix: always specify timezone: `@Scheduled(cron="0 0 * * * *", zone="UTC")`. UNSAID: `TimeZone.getDefault()` in Java returns the JVM's timezone, which may differ from the OS timezone or the Docker container's timezone (which defaults to UTC). In Kubernetes: containers default to UTC unless `TZ` environment variable is set. Cron syntax in Spring: seconds are the first field (unlike Unix cron): `second minute hour day-of-month month day-of-week`. `@Scheduled(cron="0 0 0 * * *")` — midnight every day. Spring's cron also supports `L` (last), `#` (nth weekday), `?` (no specific value for day fields). For distributed environments: combine with ShedLock to prevent double execution across instances, and always log the next scheduled execution time for debugging.

Spring Boot `ApplicationContext` testing best practices: share context across tests by ensuring the same `@SpringBootTest` configuration — different `@MockBean` sets cause context reload. Group tests by context configuration: tests sharing the same beans share the same context. `@TestConfiguration` for test-only beans without affecting production context. `ApplicationContextInitializer` for programmatic context customization in tests. `ContextCustomizerFactory` for test framework extensions. UNSAID: Spring Boot caches test application contexts — if test A uses `@MockBean UserService` and test B uses `@MockBean OrderService`, Spring creates two separate contexts. Minimize `@MockBean` usage to maximize context reuse and reduce test suite time. `@SpringBootTest(classes=MyApp.class)` explicitly specifies the configuration class — helps with multi-module projects. For test slices: `@WebMvcTest`, `@DataJpaTest`, `@JsonTest` each have their own context cache key. `@ExtendWith(SpringExtension.class)` is included in all Spring Boot test annotations — no need to add it explicitly. `ApplicationContext.publishEvent()` in tests to simulate events. `@DirtiesContext` as last resort for tests that modify shared state.

Spring Boot Elasticsearch integration with Spring Data Elasticsearch: `spring.elasticsearch.uris=http://localhost:9200`. `@Document(indexName="products") public class Product { @Id String id; @Field(type=Text, analyzer="standard") String description; @Field(type=Keyword) String category; }`. Repository extends `ElasticsearchRepository<Product, String>`. Full-text search: `repository.findByDescription(query, pageable)`. Custom query: `@Query("{\"match\": {\"description\": {\"query\": \"?0\", \"fuzziness\": \"AUTO\"}}}")`). `ElasticsearchOperations` for programmatic queries. UNSAID: Elasticsearch indexing is near-real-time (default 1s refresh interval) — don't test for immediate searchability. For production: configure `ElasticsearchClient` with connection pool, timeouts, and authentication. Index lifecycle management: define index templates and ILM policies for rolling indices (time-based log data). Spring Data Elasticsearch 5 uses the new `ElasticsearchClient` (fluent API) instead of the deprecated `RestHighLevelClient`. For complex aggregations: use `ElasticsearchOperations.search()` with `NativeQuery` + Elasticsearch Aggregation builders. Kibana + Elasticsearch for log analysis (ELK stack) — Spring Boot logs structured JSON to Logstash/Elasticsearch.

Spring Boot complete production service startup validation: (1) verify all required environment variables present at startup — fail fast; (2) test database connectivity and run migrations; (3) validate service dependencies are reachable (optional, for required dependencies); (4) warm up caches for critical data; (5) complete startup only after readiness probe passes. Pattern: `@Component class StartupValidator implements ApplicationListener<ContextRefreshedEvent> { public void onApplicationEvent(ContextRefreshedEvent event) { validateEnvVars(); warmCaches(); markReady(); } }`. UNSAID: startup validation prevents partially configured services from taking production traffic. The most important check: required secrets/API keys present (`Objects.requireNonNull(apiKey, "API_KEY must be set")`). Database migration failure must terminate startup — Spring Boot Flyway/Liquibase does this automatically. Cache warming: pre-load frequently accessed reference data (configurations, feature flags) before readiness probe passes — prevents cold-start latency spikes after deployment. Kubernetes `startupProbe` for slow-starting services: allows longer initial startup before liveness/readiness probes begin. Combine with `management.endpoint.health.probes.enabled=true` for automatic K8s probe endpoints.

Spring Boot `@Transactional` with `saveAndFlush()` vs `save()`: `JpaRepository.save()` persists the entity and adds it to the persistence context — the INSERT/UPDATE is not sent to the database until Hibernate flushes (at transaction commit or before a query that needs current state). `saveAndFlush()` immediately flushes the persistence context — the SQL is sent to the database. `saveAndFlush()` is necessary when: subsequent native queries within the same transaction need to see the saved data; when testing with `@Transactional` rollback needs to verify constraints. UNSAID: premature flushing (`saveAndFlush`) reduces Hibernate's ability to batch SQL statements — use only when necessary. Hibernate automatically flushes before executing HQL/JPQL queries that could be affected by pending changes (flush-on-query), so most code doesn't need `saveAndFlush`. `entityManager.flush()` is equivalent for arbitrary flush points. `spring.jpa.properties.hibernate.order_inserts=true` and `order_updates=true` allow Hibernate to sort and batch statements for performance — use with `hibernate.jdbc.batch_size=50`. Flush mode: `FlushMode.COMMIT` disables flush-on-query — manual flush required, risky but highest performance.

FAILURE: Spring Boot property injection order causing `@Value` to use stale values after context refresh. `@Value` fields are injected once at bean creation — they do not auto-update when properties change (e.g., after `POST /actuator/refresh` with Spring Cloud Config). Beans with `@Value` fields serve the old value after a config refresh until they're recreated. Fix: annotate beans with `@RefreshScope` to recreate them on refresh. Or use `Environment.getProperty()` for dynamic property access — reads the current value every time. UNSAID: `@RefreshScope` has a cost — the bean is recreated on every refresh event, which can interrupt in-flight requests if the bean is a stateful service. Use `@RefreshScope` on beans that are safe to recreate (stateless services, configuration holders). Avoid `@RefreshScope` on beans holding connection pools or locks. For feature flags that must update without restart: use a dedicated feature flag service (`Unleash`, `LaunchDarkly`) rather than Spring Config refresh. `Environment.getProperty()` is thread-safe for reading current values — the simplest pattern for dynamic configuration without the complexity of `@RefreshScope`.

Spring Boot `@EnableJpaAuditing` with `AuditorAware` for user tracking: `@Configuration @EnableJpaAuditing class AuditConfig { @Bean AuditorAware<String> auditorAware() { return () -> Optional.ofNullable(SecurityContextHolder.getContext().getAuthentication()).filter(a -> a.isAuthenticated() && !(a.getPrincipal() instanceof String s && s.equals("anonymousUser"))).map(Authentication::getName); } }`. Test auditing: `@WithMockUser(username="test-user") @Test void saveCreatesAuditFields()`. UNSAID: `AuditorAware` is called for every `@CreatedBy` and `@LastModifiedBy` population — keep it fast and non-blocking. For async operations (`@Async`, `@Scheduled`): `SecurityContextHolder` is empty (no authenticated user) — `AuditorAware` returns empty Optional, leaving `createdBy` null. For system-initiated auditing (batch jobs, scheduled tasks): explicitly set a system principal or use a separate service account identity. `@CreatedBy @Column(updatable=false) String createdBy` — `updatable=false` prevents overwrite on update. For UUID-based user IDs: change `AuditorAware<String>` to `AuditorAware<UUID>` and update `@CreatedBy` field type accordingly.

Spring Boot `@Bean` `RestTemplate` vs `RestClient` migration: legacy `RestTemplate` with interceptors: `restTemplate.setInterceptors(List.of(new LoggingInterceptor(), new AuthInterceptor()))`. Migrate to `RestClient`: `RestClient.builder().requestInterceptor((request, body, execution) -> { log.info("Request: {}", request.getURI()); return execution.execute(request, body); }).build()`. For existing `RestTemplate`-based code: wrap in `RestTemplateBuilder` to preserve customizations: `@Bean RestTemplate restTemplate(RestTemplateBuilder builder) { return builder.interceptors(new LoggingInterceptor()).connectTimeout(Duration.ofSeconds(5)).readTimeout(Duration.ofSeconds(30)).build(); }`. UNSAID: `RestTemplateBuilder` provided by Spring Boot auto-configuration creates a `RestTemplate` with sensible defaults and integrates with Micrometer and distributed tracing automatically. `RestTemplate` without `RestTemplateBuilder` misses these integrations. Migration priority: `RestTemplate` is in maintenance mode but not deprecated — migration is not urgent, but new code should use `RestClient`. `ClientHttpRequestInterceptor` (RestTemplate) vs `ClientHttpRequestInterceptor` (RestClient) — same interface name, different packages. `MockRestServiceServer` for testing `RestTemplate`/`RestClient` without real HTTP.

FAILURE: Spring Boot application context failing to start due to missing `@Configuration` dependencies in test slices. `@WebMvcTest` and `@DataJpaTest` only load specific layers. If your controller imports a `@Configuration` class that references a `@Service` or `@Component` not in the slice, the context fails with `NoSuchBeanDefinitionException`. Fix: use `@MockBean` for missing service dependencies, or `@Import(MissingConfig.class)` to import specific `@Configuration` classes. UNSAID: `@WebMvcTest(UserController.class)` limits scanning to `UserController` and related beans — `@Service`, `@Repository`, `@Component` outside the controller package are not scanned. `@ControllerAdvice` classes ARE included by `@WebMvcTest` (important for testing error handling). `@SecurityConfig` may need explicit import in `@WebMvcTest` if it configures security beans. For complex `@Configuration` with infrastructure beans: use `@TestConfiguration` to provide test-specific bean definitions that override production beans. A common pattern: a `TestSecurityConfig.class` with `permitAll()` for all endpoints, imported in tests that don't test security. `@AutoConfigureMockMvc(addFilters=false)` as a simpler alternative to configuring security in tests.

Spring Boot `@Async` with `CompletableFuture` for parallel service calls: `@Async CompletableFuture<UserProfile> getProfile(Long id) { return CompletableFuture.completedFuture(userService.findById(id)); }`. `@Async CompletableFuture<List<Order>> getOrders(Long userId) { return CompletableFuture.completedFuture(orderService.findByUserId(userId)); }`. In orchestrator: `CompletableFuture<UserProfile> profile = profileService.getProfile(id); CompletableFuture<List<Order>> orders = orderService.getOrders(id); CompletableFuture.allOf(profile, orders).join(); return new UserDashboard(profile.get(), orders.get())`. UNSAID: both `getProfile` and `getOrders` execute concurrently because they're started before `join()`. Without `@Async`, sequential execution takes sum of both latencies; with `@Async`, it takes max of both latencies. `CompletableFuture.allOf()` waits for all to complete — check `future.isCompletedExceptionally()` for error handling. `thenCombine`, `thenCompose` for non-blocking composition. Spring Boot 3 + virtual threads: `@Async` with `newVirtualThreadPerTaskExecutor()` — each async call gets its own virtual thread at near-zero cost. For WebFlux: `Mono.zip(profileMono, ordersMono)` for reactive parallel composition without thread switching.

Spring Boot JPA `@Column` mapping best practices: `@Column(name="email", nullable=false, unique=true, length=255)`. For long text: `@Column(columnDefinition="TEXT")`. For JSON: `@Column(columnDefinition="JSONB")` with `@Convert(converter=JsonConverter.class)`. For `Instant`: `@Column(columnDefinition="TIMESTAMPTZ")` (PostgreSQL timestamp with timezone). For monetary values: `@Column(precision=19, scale=4)` with `BigDecimal`. UNSAID: `@Column` attributes like `nullable=false` and `unique=true` are used by Hibernate's DDL generation (`spring.jpa.hibernate.ddl-auto=create` or `validate`) and by JPA's schema validation — they do NOT add Java-level null checks. Bean Validation `@NotNull` is separate and enforced at the application layer. Always use database-level constraints (NOT NULL, UNIQUE) AND application-level validation (`@NotNull`, `@Email`) — defense in depth. `@Column(insertable=false, updatable=false)` for columns managed by the database (generated columns, computed values). `@Generated(GenerationTime.INSERT)` for database-generated values (like `DEFAULT now()`). Avoid `columnDefinition` for cross-database compatibility — use standard JPA annotations where possible.

Spring Boot `@ControllerAdvice` response body normalization with `ResponseBodyAdvice<T>`: `@RestControllerAdvice public class ResponseWrapper implements ResponseBodyAdvice<Object> { @Override public boolean supports(MethodParameter mp, Class converter) { return !mp.getParameterType().equals(ResponseEntity.class); } @Override public Object beforeBodyWrite(Object body, MethodParameter mp, MediaType type, Class converter, ServerHttpRequest req, ServerHttpResponse res) { return new ApiResponse<>("success", body); } }`. UNSAID: `ResponseBodyAdvice` wraps all controller responses in a uniform format — useful for consistent API responses: `{"status": "success", "data": {...}}`. Limitations: conflicts with `Page<T>` responses from Spring Data (the page metadata gets wrapped), and with error responses (handle separately in `@ExceptionHandler`). Skip specific controllers: check `mp.getContainingClass()`. Spring Framework 6+: prefer `HttpMessageConverter` composition or explicit response wrapping in controllers for clarity. RFC 7807 Problem Details is the standard for error responses — use it instead of custom error wrappers. For success responses: explicit `ResponseEntity<ApiResponse<T>>` at each controller is more explicit but verbose.

Spring Boot `@Bean` lifecycle: `InitializingBean.afterPropertiesSet()` vs `@PostConstruct`: `@PostConstruct` is the standard JSR-250 annotation — preferred. `InitializingBean` is Spring-specific and couples the bean to Spring framework. `DisposableBean.destroy()` vs `@PreDestroy`: same preference for `@PreDestroy`.
